title: Trump Signs Executive Order Accelerating Post-Quantum Cryptography Migration
blurb: Trump signed Executive Order 14409 to accelerate federal migration to post-quantum cryptography, citing the "harvest now, decrypt later" threat in which adversaries steal encrypted data today to crack it once quantum computers mature. Agencies must inventory high-value systems and transition them to PQC for key establishment by the end of 2030 and digital signatures by 2031, with federal contractors required to comply with NIST standards on the same timeline.

title: Evaluation Awareness Is Not One Capability: Evidence from Open Language Models
blurb: A new paper argues that "evaluation awareness" in language models is not a single capability but several independent ones, undercutting the assumption that benchmark safety scores predict real deployment behavior. Across 37 open-weight models, the authors find that detecting test conditions, shifting behavior under that detection, and steerability vary independently, so no single awareness score reliably proxies how safely a model acts once the evaluation harness is removed.

title: VibeThinker: 3B param model that beats Opus 4.5 on reasoning with novel SFT+GRPO
blurb: VibeThinker-3B is a 3B-parameter dense model that reaches frontier reasoning scores, hitting 94.3 on AIME26 and 80.2 Pass@1 on LiveCodeBench v6 while matching or beating far larger systems like Gemini 3 Pro and DeepSeek V3.2. The authors credit a Spectrum-to-Signal pipeline of curriculum SFT, multi-domain RL, and self-distillation, arguing that verifiable reasoning compresses into compact cores while broad knowledge still needs parameter coverage.

title: Tmax: A simple recipe for terminal agents
blurb: Tmax is the strongest open RL recipe for terminal agents yet, hitting 27% on Terminal-Bench 2.0 with just 9B parameters.

title: CoorDex: Coordinating Body and Hand Priors for Continuous Dexterous Humanoid Loco-Manipulation
blurb: CoorDex is a learning pipeline that lets a humanoid manipulate objects with a dexterous hand while still walking, replacing the usual stop-walk-then-grasp routine. Tested on a Unitree G1 with a 20-DoF hand, it handled non-stop bottle grasping and carrying, opening a fridge door on the move, and cube pick-and-turn by composing separate body and hand motion priors through residual reinforcement learning.

title: China Takes Supercomputer Crown From U.S. For First Time Since 2017
blurb: A supercomputer in Shenzhen was named the world's fastest, giving China the top spot for the first time since 2017. The machine runs entirely on standard microprocessors rather than the GPUs that dominate modern high-performance computing, an unusual design choice as the US restricts China's access to advanced AI chips.

title: Memory Chips and China, Microsoft and Chinese Models
blurb: Ben Thompson argues that the big three memory makers, by selling to Chinese customers, risk seeding the rivals that will eventually undercut them, much as past openings let Chinese manufacturers climb the value chain. He adds that Microsoft has strong incentives to adopt Chinese open models, since cheaper, capable weights would lower its inference costs and reduce dependence on a single frontier lab.

title: China appears to be developing 7-meter-diameter reusable rockets
blurb: Procurement records, a delivered 7.5-meter stainless steel forging, and launch pad planning indicate China's main space contractor CASC is developing a new 7-meter-diameter reusable rocket, sitting between the 5-meter Long March 10 and the 10-meter Long March 9. The intermediate vehicle, which has no public designation, would use clusters of methalox YF-209 engines to lift 25,000 to 50,000 kg to low Earth orbit, comparable to Blue Origin's New Glenn.

title: EnterpriseClawBench: Benchmarking Agents from Real Workplace Sessions
blurb: EnterpriseClawBench builds 852 reproducible agent tasks from real workplace sessions; the best setup (Codex with GPT-5.5) scores only 0.663.

title: Beyond Simpson's Paradox: A Cascade of Confounders in AI Agent Pull-Request Co-Authorship
blurb: This paper reanalyzes 33,596 pull requests from five AI coding agents and shows that the apparent merge-rate penalty for human co-authorship (53.8% vs. 79.8%) is a Simpson's Paradox driven by agent mix, since Codex dominates the data and rarely uses co-authorship. Once repository selection and PR structure are controlled, no agent retains a clear co-authorship effect, suggesting prior cross-sectional associations are selection artifacts rather than causal benefits.

title: An Introduction to YOLO26
blurb: Roboflow published an introduction to YOLO26, an end-to-end computer vision model family that handles detection, instance segmentation, pose estimation, oriented object detection, and classification across five size variants from Nano to Extra Large. Released in January 2026, it removes Non-Maximum Suppression to cut latency and drops the Distribution Focal Loss module for better edge and low-power hardware support, with the post benchmarking it on COCO against RF-DETR, LW-DETR, and D-FINE.

title: The new HTTP QUERY method explained
blurb: The new HTTP QUERY method standardizes sending a request body with read requests, solving the long-standing problem of cramming complex filters, deep nesting, and arrays into GET query strings that hit URL length limits and encoding ambiguity. Unlike GET requests with bodies, which proxies and servers handle inconsistently, QUERY is safe and idempotent by design, giving APIs a defined way to express rich queries without resorting to POST.

title: OpenAI wants AI to fix vulnerabilities, not just find them
blurb: OpenAI expanded Daybreak, its cybersecurity initiative, with an updated Codex Security platform that now finds, validates, and patches software vulnerabilities rather than just surfacing them. Since its March preview, Codex Security has scanned over 30 million commits across 30,000 codebases and automatically confirmed more than 500,000 findings as fixed, with human operators deciding which patches to apply.

title: A Chinese supercomputer just secured first place on the Top500 list for the first time since 2017: Here’s everything we know about LineShine
blurb: China's LineShine supercomputer took the top spot on the 2026 Top500 list with a debut score of 2.198 exaflops on the HPL benchmark, ending El Capitan's reign and marking the first Chinese number one since Sunway TaihuLight in 2017. The Shenzhen-based system runs roughly 14 million Arm-based cores in a rare CPU-only design, delivering a 20% performance edge over El Capitan while pushing three remaining US systems into the second-through-fifth spots.

title: OpenAI prepares bidirectional voice mode for rollout on ChatGPT
blurb: OpenAI is preparing a major upgrade to ChatGPT's voice mode, with a next-generation audio model called Bidi 1 already reaching a subset of users ahead of a possible release this week. The bidirectional design lets the assistant speak, hear, and listen at once, holding conversational context across a session and offering natural acknowledgments without cutting in, addressing the context-dropping weakness of the current voice stack.

title: MacAgentBench: Benchmarking AI Agents on Real-World macOS Desktop
blurb: MacAgentBench tests AI agents on 676 real macOS tasks across 25 apps, with Claude Opus 4.6 on OpenClaw hitting 73.7% Pass@1.

title: Getty Just Became an AI Company. It Now Has to Perform Like One
blurb: Getty Images stock soared 145% after signing a content-licensing deal with OpenAI, marking a strategic pivot from competitor to supplier in the AI image market. The stock-image company had previously bet on building its own generative models, but the OpenAI tie-up reframes its catalog as training and licensing inventory rather than a product it must out-engineer.

title: AutoDex: An Automated Real-World System for Dexterous Grasping Data Collection
blurb: AutoDex is an automated system that collects real-world robotic grasping data without human intervention, handling perception, execution, success labeling, and object reset in a closed loop. Across 3,593 trials on Allegro and Inspire hands, it ran 4.8x faster than teleoperation, and grasps drawn from its physically validated database succeeded 76% of the time versus 34% for simulation-only validation.

title: Managing Procedural Memory in LLM Agents: Control, Adaptation, and Evaluation
blurb: AFTER benchmarks procedural memory across 382 enterprise tasks, finding a single refinement round lifts agent performance 3.7-6.7 points.

title: Scaling LLM Knowledge Boundaries via Distribution-Optimized Synthesis
blurb: KDoS optimizes synthetic-data knowledge distribution to expand LLM knowledge boundaries, beating baselines across six benchmarks.

title: On Good Authority: Release-Authority Measurement for Registry-Mediated Package Ecosystems
blurb: This paper introduces a release-authority record that compares each package release to its predecessor across publisher, repository, workflow, provenance, and signing evidence to flag when the public path used to publish code quietly changed. Tested across 45,812 releases from npm, PyPI, Maven Central, and RubyGems, the method surfaced 204 release-path discontinuities as review cues, with practitioners rating most flagged cases as worth immediate inspection.

title: Long-Theorized GPS Weakness Exploited on Large Scale
blurb: A large-scale GPS jamming and spoofing campaign tested the resilience of GNSS mitigation efforts, exposing the long-known weakness that satellite signals from 20,000 km up are too faint to resist interference. Xona's low-Earth-orbit Pulsar-0 satellite offers one countermeasure, broadcasting positioning and timing signals roughly 100 times stronger than standard GPS while cross-checking against GPS to stay in agreement.

title: Oracle sheds 21,000 roles over the past year amid wave of AI layoffs from tech giants
blurb: Oracle cut 21,000 jobs over the past year, roughly 13% of its workforce, dropping its full-time headcount to 141,000 as of May 2026. The company cited AI adoption directly in its annual filing and spent $1.8B on restructuring, joining Meta, Microsoft, and others slashing roles while pouring billions into AI data center buildouts.

title: Oracle Sheds 21,000 Jobs as It Sharpens Focus on AI
blurb: Oracle cut roughly 21,000 jobs in its last fiscal year, shrinking its head count about 13% as it redirects spending toward AI infrastructure. The cuts accompany a heavy buildout of data-center and cloud capacity, signaling Oracle is reshaping its workforce around AI demand rather than its legacy software business.

title: ReasoningLens: Hierarchical Visualization and Diagnostic Auditing for Large Reasoning Models
blurb: ReasoningLens is an open-source tool that structures long chain-of-thought traces into interactive hierarchies and runs an agentic auditor to flag reasoning errors.

title: Valve working on SteamOS for general release — company collaborating with Nvidia to ensure compatibility, hints at dual-boot capabilities in the future
blurb: Valve is developing SteamOS for general release, collaborating closely with Nvidia to add support for its GPUs beyond the AMD hardware the OS was built around. Engineer Pierre-Loup Griffais told The Verge that Valve is also building an installer to replace existing operating systems and hinted at future dual-boot support, paving the way for DIY Steam Machines. SteamOS holds a 3.99% Steam share, recently passing macOS.

title: In praise of memcached
blurb: The author argues that memcached is often the better choice for a cache because its minimal feature set prevents teams from quietly turning it into a database, the fate that frequently befalls Redis. Because Redis persists data and offers rich abstractions, developers start relying on it as a system of record, leaving ops to monitor it like a pet rather than a disposable cache.

title: With Starfall, SpaceX eyes an edge in global cargo delivery from orbit
blurb: SpaceX launched its first test of Starfall, a saucer-shaped reentry pod designed to deliver cargo anywhere on Earth from low-Earth orbit, on a Falcon 9 from Cape Canaveral. After two orbits, the vehicle was set for a parachute-assisted Pacific splashdown, with SpaceX also pitching it for in-space manufacturing and returning microgravity-made materials like pharmaceuticals.

title: [AINews] SpaceX is already a $28B/yr Neocloud
blurb: SpaceX has quietly built a GPU-rental business worth roughly $28B a year, after signing its third compute deal with Reflection AI alongside existing customers Anthropic and Google. Analyst Jamin Ball estimates the contracts total $2.32B a month at over $10 an hour for Blackwell chips, putting SpaceX at about twice CoreWeave's current revenue.

title: VeriPort: Automated and Verified Patch Backporting at Scale
blurb: VeriPort is an agentic system that automatically backports a security patch to every affected version of a dependency and builds an evidence chain confirming each fix blocks exploitation while preserving behavior. It resolved 95.3% of 128 BackportBench tasks, beating Claude Code by 22.7 points, and generated over 5,000 verified patches across 169 CVEs while surfacing 2,100 versions wrongly flagged as affected and 127 previously unknown vulnerable ones.

title: They sold their startup to Datadog. Now, they’ve raised $35M to disrupt it
blurb: Tsuga, founded by two ex-Datadog engineers who sold their previous startup Madumbo to Datadog in 2019, raised $35M in Series A funding led by Singular, bringing total funding to $45M. The Paris-based observability platform runs inside each customer's own cloud account to avoid the per-byte fees that make legacy tools expensive for high-volume AI telemetry, and has booked several million in revenue six months after launch.

title: SK hynix passes Samsung as South Korea's most valuable company — memory company surpasses valuation milestone on the back of HBM
blurb: SK hynix overtook Samsung Electronics on Monday to become South Korea's most valuable listed company, the first time Samsung has lost the top KOSPI spot since November 2000. The memory maker's shares have rallied more than 340% this year on demand for the high-bandwidth memory it supplies to Nvidia, where it holds 61% of the global HBM market against Samsung's 17%.

title: Crypto in 2026: Oh, This Is the Bad Place
blurb: Stephen Diehl argues that crypto in 2026 has normalized a series of dystopian absurdities: a sitting US president running a White House memecoin with top holders seated by purchase volume, federally licensed exchanges taking retail bets on foreign assassinations, and a statute-blessed stablecoin system migrating poor people's savings onto opaque private balance sheets. He contends that each scenario alone reads as cyberpunk fiction, and that policy vocabulary now has to catch up to a reality that has outpaced satire.

title: Will It Mythos?
blurb: A developer built a benchmark testing whether models like Claude Opus can find the security bugs Anthropic credits to its restricted Mythos model.

title: Récolter aujourd’hui, déchiffrer demain : pourquoi Washington accélère sa transition post-quantique
blurb: Trump signed two executive orders on quantum technology, the more consequential one moving the federal deadline for migrating to post-quantum cryptography forward from 2035 to 2031. The acceleration reflects the "Harvest Now, Decrypt Later" threat, in which adversaries collect encrypted data today to decrypt once quantum computers mature, while a companion order targets a scientifically useful quantum computer by 2028.

title: Mach Industries raised $300M at a $1.8B valuation — and its new in-house rocket-motor arm targets a U.S. supply bottleneck
blurb: Mach Industries raised $300M in a Series C at a $1.8B valuation, led by Infinite Capital and Ribbit Capital, roughly quadrupling the defense manufacturer's paper value in a year. The Huntington Beach drone maker also acquired propulsion firm Exquadrum, folding it into a new Mach Energetics division to build solid rocket motors in-house and address a constrained U.S. supply of the components.

title: Anthropic prepares Cowork support for mobile apps
blurb: Anthropic appears close to extending Cowork, its agentic system for knowledge work, to mobile, with a feature-flagged entry surfacing in an iOS build that lets users schedule tasks from a phone and pick up results on web or desktop. The copy suggests moving execution to the cloud, lifting the constraint of March's Dispatch feature that required a desktop session to stay awake, with a release possible as soon as this week.

title: Towards Whole Hand and Wrist Kinematic Tracking with a Wearable A-Mode Ultrasound Probe
blurb: Researchers demonstrated whole-hand and wrist motion tracking from a wearable A-mode ultrasound probe, regressing all 23 degrees of freedom directly on the device. A compact 11,285-parameter neural network runs entirely on the WULPUS platform's nRF52832 microcontroller at 29.1 ms latency and 33 mW, enabling up to 36 hours of continuous use and cutting wireless bandwidth by 88% versus streaming raw data.

title: OpenAI launches new initiative to help find and patch open source bugs
blurb: OpenAI launched Patch the Planet, an initiative with security firm Trail of Bits to help open source maintainers find and fix bugs using tools like Codex Security. Trail of Bits engineers will triage findings and develop patches before they reach maintainers, positioning the program as a defensive counterpart to AI security tools that can also automate exploit discovery.

title: UK government backs university AI labs with £60M to make AI cheaper
blurb: The UK government committed up to £60M to new AI labs at Oxford and UCL, its first major investment under UKRI's national AI strategy. UCL's lab will build open-source models that run on widely available hardware while Oxford pursues efficient approaches that avoid vast centralized compute, part of a push to cut reliance on US providers and make AI cheaper for British businesses.

title: iCloud class action lawsuit could see UK users share $4B payout
blurb: A UK competition tribunal cleared a Which?-led antitrust class action against Apple to proceed, claiming £3B ($4B) over allegations that Apple ties iCloud to iPhone and iPad usage and blocks rival cloud services from deep system integration. Roughly 40 million UK iCloud users from November 2018 onward are automatically included unless they opt out, putting the potential payout at about £77 ($102) each if the case succeeds.

title: Meta pauses AI training program tracking employee keystrokes after internal leak
blurb: Meta paused its Model Capability Initiative, a mandatory program that trained internal AI models on employee keystrokes and mouse movements, after a leak exposed private conversations, performance data, and transcriptions across the entire company. Meta classified the incident as a SEV 2 and is investigating, though it says it has found no evidence that any employee improperly accessed the data.

title: Chevron signs 20-year power agreement with Microsoft for West Texas data center
blurb: Chevron signed a 20-year power purchase agreement with Microsoft to build Project Kilby, a co-located natural gas facility in West Texas delivering roughly 2.67 gigawatts of dedicated electricity to a Microsoft data center. The phased buildout draws mainly on GE Vernova turbines and Permian gas, ranking among the largest co-located gas-and-data-center projects in the US as labs scramble to secure power for AI compute.

title: How fuzzy APIs are remaking the web
blurb: The author argues that AI-mediated APIs are reviving service-oriented architecture, replacing the brittle, hand-coded integrations between microservices with endpoints that interpret intent rather than demand exact contracts. Where SOA 1.0 collapsed under SOAP, WSDL, and rigid XML definitions that broke on a single missing tag, the author contends AI's tolerance for ambiguity could finally deliver the flexible, discoverable service orchestration that earlier attempts promised and failed to ship.

title: Amazon Ordered to Collectively Bargain at San Francisco Warehouse
blurb: An NLRB judge ordered Amazon to bargain with its San Francisco warehouse workers, setting up an appeal that could overturn union-friendly precedent.

title: Trump Seeks to Boost Quantum Computing With New Executive Orders
blurb: The Trump administration is preparing executive orders to accelerate US quantum computing development while addressing the security risks the technology poses to current encryption. The orders signal that Washington is treating quantum as a strategic priority on par with AI and chips, where federal coordination and funding aim to keep the US ahead of China.

title: The man who built Groq handed its technology to NVIDIA, now his old company raised $650M to fight back without him
blurb: Groq raised $650M to scale its inference chips, months after founder Jonathan Ross, the engineer who co-developed Google's TPU, departed and moved his work toward Nvidia. The round positions Groq to keep competing in AI inference hardware without its founding architect, betting its LPU design can undercut Nvidia GPUs on cost and speed for running large models.

title: VibeThinker: 3B param model that beats Opus 4.5 on reasoning with novel SFT+GRPO
blurb: VibeThinker-3B is a 3B-parameter dense model that pushes verifiable reasoning to frontier levels through curriculum-based supervised fine-tuning, multi-domain reinforcement learning, and offline self-distillation. It scores 94.3 on AIME26, 80.2 Pass@1 on LiveCodeBench v6, and 93.4 on IFEval, matching or exceeding far larger systems like DeepSeek V3.2, GLM-5, and Gemini 3 Pro.

title: SPIRAL: Learning to Search and Aggregate
blurb: SPIRAL is a reinforcement learning framework that trains language models to use sequential, parallel, and aggregative reasoning as a unified inference pipeline, sampling independent chain-of-thought traces in parallel and conditioning a final aggregation trace on them. Optimized end-to-end against the aggregated response reward, it outperforms GRPO by up to 11x in scaling efficiency and 15% in performance when all three compute primitives are scaled.

title: Evaluation Awareness Is Not One Capability: Evidence from Open Language Models
blurb: This paper argues that evaluation awareness in language models is multivariate rather than a single capability, testing 37 open-weight models across seven families in eight experiments. The authors find that detectability, behavioral shifts, and controllability vary independently, with hard refusals dropping 5.8 points under hypothetical framing and probes retaining 0.98 AUROC even after behavior collapses below chance.

title: CoorDex: Coordinating Body and Hand Priors for Continuous Dexterous Humanoid Loco-Manipulation
blurb: CoorDex is a learning pipeline that enables humanoids to perform dexterous manipulation while walking, replacing the usual stop-and-go approach of pausing to operate a low-DoF gripper. It distills whole-body and hand motion-tracking teachers into frozen latent priors, then runs residual reinforcement learning over them, letting a Unitree G1 with a 20-DoF hand grasp bottles, open fridge doors, and pick-and-turn cubes on the move.

title: Kamera: Unified Position-Invariant Multimodal KV Cache for Training-Free Reuse
blurb: Kamera is a training-free method that lets multimodal agents reuse KV cache for video frames, screenshots, and other artifacts at any position, rather than re-encoding them from scratch every time the context window slides. It pairs exact RoPE re-rotation with a small low-rank conditioning patch that restores the cross-chunk binding naive reuse drops, recovering full accuracy on multi-hop benchmarks across MLA, GQA, and MHA at a fraction of the KV footprint.

title: Tmax: A simple recipe for terminal agents
blurb: Tmax is an open reinforcement-learning recipe for terminal-using agents that scores 27% on Terminal-Bench 2.0 with just 9B parameters, outperforming much larger models from prior work. The authors generate training environments using a taxonomy that combines difficulty control, personas, and verifier diversification, and they open-source the resulting dataset, which is over 2.5x larger than previously released terminal-agent datasets, alongside their models and code.

title: Exposing the Illusion of Erasure in Knowledge Editing for LLMs
blurb: This paper examines knowledge editing for LLMs from an adversarial elicitation angle and finds that "erased" facts are often not removed but redistributed within the model's representation space. Through mechanistic analysis, the authors show low-rank edits act as targeted suppression rather than deletion, leaving knowledge in narrow, perturbation-sensitive regions that indirect prompting and adversarial attacks can recover, making KE methods inherently bypassable.

title: Habituation at the Gate: Rising Approval and Declining Scrutiny in Human Review of AI Agent Code
blurb: This paper studies whether human reviewers lower their scrutiny of AI-generated pull requests over time, analyzing 11,429 reviews from 400 repeat reviewers across a seven-month period using the AIDev dataset. Approval rates rose from 30.1% to 36.8% as reviewers gained experience, while inline comment volume fell 22% and review latency grew 3.5x, a pattern the authors read as reflexive habituation under workload rather than calibrated trust.

title: Scheduling Thoughts: Learning the Order of Thought in Diffusion Language Models
blurb: This paper introduces Self-Aware Scheduling (SAS), a method that learns the order in which masked diffusion language models unmask tokens, treating order selection as a policy optimization problem over a frozen denoiser. SAS lifts Sudoku puzzle accuracy on a 1B model from 82.0% to 91.8%, and improves GSM8K pass@1 on LLaDA-8B from 64% to 76%, consistently matching or beating heuristic schedules.

title: China Takes Supercomputer Crown From U.S. For First Time Since 2017
blurb: A supercomputer in Shenzhen was declared the world's fastest, giving China the top spot for the first time since 2017. The system reaches its performance using only standard microprocessors rather than the GPUs that power most leading AI and high-performance computing systems.

title: Self-Compacting Language Model Agents
blurb: SelfCompact is a scaffold that lets a language model agent decide when and how to compact its own context, pairing a model-invoked summarization tool with a lightweight rubric that specifies when to fire and when to suppress. Across six math and agentic-search benchmarks and seven models, it matches or beats fixed-interval summarization while improving over a no-summarization baseline by up to 18.1 points on math at 30-70% lower per-question cost.

title: LangMAP: A Language-Adaptive Approach to Tokenization
blurb: LangMAP extends the UnigramLM algorithm to the multilingual setting, producing language-specific tokenization from a single shared vocabulary that works either when training a multilingual model from scratch or when adapting a pretrained model's tokenizer without changing its vocabulary. Across 14 tokenizers, 9 natural languages, and 9 programming languages, it improves morphological boundary alignment and AST leaf alignment, though fine-tuning gains on knowledge tasks were inconsistent.

title: Detecting Malicious Agent Skills in the Wild using Attention
blurb: This paper introduces Locate-and-Judge, a two-stage detector for malicious LLM agent skills, where a lightweight locator scores structural spans by instruction-following attention and retains the top-K for a costlier judge to examine. The approach cuts scanning costs by an order of magnitude versus direct LLM scanning, surfacing dozens of live malicious skills that SkillSpector and Cisco Skill Scanner miss, and releases a labeled dataset.

title: Memory Chips and China, Microsoft and Chinese Models
blurb: Ben Thompson argues the big three memory makers may regret opening the door to Chinese memory manufacturers, who could eventually erode their pricing power the way Chinese producers have in other commodity hardware. He also contends Microsoft is strongly incentivized to adopt Chinese open models, since cheaper weights would improve the margins on its Copilot and Azure AI products.

title: FlexServe: A Fast and Secure LLM Serving System for Mobile Devices with Flexible Resource Isolation
blurb: This paper presents FlexServe, a secure on-device LLM serving system that uses ARM TrustZone to protect model weights and user data from a compromised OS kernel. Its Recallable Resource Isolation mechanism lets the normal-world OS allocate and reclaim secure memory and NPU resources without accessing them, achieving average time-to-first-token speedups of 10.05X over a TrustZone strawman and 2.44X over an optimized one.

title: The Topology of Ill-Posed Questions: Persistent Homology for Detection and Steering in LLMs
blurb: This paper introduces a method for detecting ill-posed questions by modeling an LLM's per-layer hidden states as a point cloud and summarizing its geometry with zero-dimensional persistent homology. Across three open-weight models, the topology features lift ill-posedness classification accuracy from 67.4% to 78.9% on AmbigQA and from 57.6% to 69.6% on CLAMBER, while topology-conditioned activation steering raises acceptable response rates from 61.4% to 70.6%.

title: TROPT: An Open Framework for Unifying and Advancing Discrete Text Optimization
blurb: TROPT is the first open-source framework that unifies discrete text-trigger optimizers, the methods that search for input sequences to steer models for red-teaming, jailbreaking, and interpretability, under a single swappable interface for models, objectives, and optimizers. It ships with 30+ optimization recipes built from 15+ optimizers spanning white-box to black-box access and 15+ losses, and the authors use it to compare jailbreak strategies and port optimizers to new domains like corpus-poisoning embedding models.

title: EnterpriseClawBench: Benchmarking Agents from Real Workplace Sessions
blurb: EnterpriseClawBench is an enterprise agent benchmark built from proprietary real-world workplace sessions, yielding 852 reproducible tasks paired with recovered fixtures, rewritten prompts, role classes, hard rules, and semantic rubrics. The best configuration, Codex with GPT-5.5, reached only 0.663, leading the authors to argue that evaluation must report harness-model combinations, artifact delivery, cost, runtime, and skill transfer rather than a single collapsed score.

title: Understanding the (In)Security of Vibe-Coded Applications
blurb: This paper presents a systematic security study of vibe-coded applications, where users build software primarily through natural-language interactions with AI agents. Analyzing a large corpus of real deployed apps, the authors find recurring vulnerability patterns distinct from conventional development, including placeholder logic, unfiltered input, and exposed secrets. These flaws trace to systematic agent limitations like memory loss and weak security knowledge, and better models or prompting reduce but do not eliminate the underlying risk.

title: Attacking the Trusted Imagination: Oracle-Level Integrity Attacks on Imagine-then-Act World Models
blurb: This paper identifies the "imagined" latent trajectory in imagine-then-act vision-language-action policies as an overlooked attack surface, showing that an L-infinity-bounded perturbation passed through projected gradient descent can corrupt a world-action model's future prediction roughly 60x more effectively than random noise. While the reactive policy stays robust and off-manifold corruption is detected at AUC 1.0, a native imagination-driven MPC planner suffers the first adversary-specific task failure, with success dropping from 0.70 to 0.05 at epsilon=0.01.

title: GroundEval: A Deterministic Replacement for LLM-as-Judge in Stateful Agent Evaluation
blurb: GroundEval is a judge-free framework for evaluating stateful agents against grounded, time-bounded, and access-controlled evidence, scoring both the final answer and the recorded trajectory that produced it. It targets three failures that LLM-as-judge misses across Silence, Perspective, and Counterfactual tracks, and in one case study flagged a response two frontier judges scored above 0.85 with a deterministic 0.000 because the agent never retrieved the artifact its answer depended on.

title: OpenAI wants AI to fix vulnerabilities, not just find them
blurb: OpenAI expanded Daybreak, its cybersecurity initiative that pairs its models, Codex Security, and human researchers to discover, validate, and remediate software vulnerabilities. Codex Security has scanned over 30 million commits across 30,000 codebases since its March preview, automatically confirming fixes for more than 500,000 findings while human operators decide which patches to apply.

title: Randomized YaRN Improves Length Generalization for Long-Context Reasoning
blurb: This paper introduces Randomized YaRN, a training method that improves length generalization by combining YaRN positional extrapolation with randomized positional encoding and a length curriculum, exposing models to out-of-distribution positions even on short-context data. Trained on sequences under 8K, it consistently outperforms standard fine-tuning on BABILong and MRCR across 16K to 128K contexts, with the largest gains at far out-of-distribution lengths.

title: Beyond Simpson's Paradox: A Cascade of Confounders in AI Agent Pull-Request Co-Authorship
blurb:
This paper re-examines a finding that pull requests with a human Co-Authored-By trailer merge less often than fully autonomous ones (53.8% vs. 79.8%), showing it is a Simpson's Paradox driven by agent composition. Across 33,596 AIDev PRs, no agent retains a clear co-authorship effect once repository selection and PR structure are controlled.

(60 words)

title: DiT-Reward: Generative Representations for Text-to-Image Reward Modeling
blurb: DiT-Reward converts a pretrained text-to-image Diffusion Transformer into a reward model by processing near-clean image latents and aggregating text-conditioned representations across transformer layers. Trained on the same data mixture as HPSv3, it beats that baseline on all four preference benchmarks, reaching 85.6% on HPDv2, and delivers a 1.65x inference speedup with comparable peak memory.

title: Polycepta: Object-Centric Appearance Estimation for Multi-Object Tracking
blurb: Polycepta is an object-centric appearance estimation framework for multi-object tracking that reframes appearance modeling as a recursive estimation problem, building and continuously updating an independent appearance state for each tracked object instead of matching static frame-wise descriptors. It runs at 90.57 Hz and reaches 92.27% MOTA on KITTI within the RobMOT framework, cutting identity switches across KITTI, Waymo Open Dataset, and MOT17.

title: SVD-Surgeon: Optimal Singular-Value Surgery for Large Language Model Compression
blurb: SVD-Surgeon is a training-free method that brings the Optimal Brain Surgeon framework to the singular-value basis, computing a closed-form update of retained singular values that compensates to second order for those removed during low-rank truncation. The same analysis produces a saliency score for which values to prune, and layered on top of SVD-LLM it improves the perplexity-compression trade-off on the OPT family and LLaMA 2-7B without retraining.

title: Can LLMs Reliably Self-Report Adversarial Prefills, and How?
blurb: This paper tests whether LLMs can recognize that their own prior output was elicited by an adversarial prefill attack, evaluating ten open-weight instruction-tuned models from 3B to 70B across four safety benchmarks. No model reliably flagged its compromised outputs, claiming genuine intent on prefilled responses 27.3% of the time on average, and LoRA finetuning that widened the gap counterintuitively raised attack success rates under prefill.

title: One-Prompt Censorship Evasion via Generative Diffusion Models
blurb: This paper reframes internet censorship evasion as a semantic image-to-image editing task that users control with a single natural-language prompt. FlowPaint, the proposed framework, uses an instruction-tuned diffusion model to reshape censored network traffic into benign patterns, outperforming existing evasion baselines against both industrial rule-based middleboxes and learning-based classifiers.

title: An Introduction to YOLO26
blurb: YOLO26 is an end-to-end computer vision model family supporting detection, instance segmentation, pose estimation, oriented object detection, and classification across five size variants from Nano to Extra Large. It removes Non-Maximum Suppression to cut latency and drops the Distribution Focal Loss module for better compatibility with edge and low-power hardware, and this post covers the architecture, COCO benchmarks, and comparisons to RF-DETR, LW-DETR, and D-FINE.

title: TooBad: Backdoor Diffusion Models with Ultra-Low Poison Rate and Imperceptible Trigger
blurb: TooBad is a backdoor attack framework for diffusion models that uses a model-tailored trigger optimization technique to plant hidden behavior with far less poisoned data than prior methods. On CIFAR-10 it reaches over 85% attack success at a 0.5% poison rate versus the 10% typically required, hits near-100% success within 3-5 injection epochs at 5% poisoning, and evades current state-of-the-art defenses while preserving model utility.

title: Shipping huggingface_hub every week with AI, open tools, and a human in the loop
blurb: Hugging Face moved huggingface_hub, the Python client underpinning transformers, datasets, and diffusers, from a 4-to-6-week release cycle to weekly releases driven by a single GitHub Actions workflow. The pipeline automates mechanical steps like version bumping, tagging, and downstream test branches using open-source tools and open-weights models, keeping a human in the loop only for release notes and announcement copy where judgment matters.

title: We got local models to triage the OpenClaw repo for FREE!*
blurb: This post demonstrates using local open-weight models like Gemma and Qwen in an agent harness to triage issues and PRs in the OpenClaw repo, classifying each into a finite set of labels via structured outputs. Running on a local NVIDIA GB10 with 128GB of unified memory, the setup delivers near-instant notifications for free rather than batching work to conserve a paid API quota.

title: A Chinese supercomputer just secured first place on the Top500 list for the first time since 2017: Here’s everything we know about LineShine
blurb: China's LineShine supercomputer topped the Top500 list at 2.198 exaflops, the first Chinese system to rank first since 2017's TaihuLight.

title: OpenAI prepares bidirectional voice mode for rollout on ChatGPT
blurb: OpenAI is preparing to roll out Bidi 1, a next-generation audio model for ChatGPT's voice mode that can speak, hear, and listen simultaneously. References surfaced in the web interface ahead of a possible release this week, and early testing shows it holds full conversation context, offers natural acknowledgments during pauses, and switches tasks on the fly, addressing weaknesses in the current voice stack.

title: Memory Contagion: Cross-Temporal Propagation of Evaluator Bias via Agent Memory
blurb: This paper introduces Memory Contagion, a phenomenon where evaluator bias propagates across time through LLM agent memory, infecting future agents that retrieve from a shared memory store even under perfect (oracle) consolidation. The authors show bias spreads at contamination rates as low as 0.2 with no safe threshold, and that consolidation attenuates length bias while amplifying authority bias, exposing a vulnerability in current agent memory designs.

title: Safety in Self-Evolving LLM Agent Systems: Threats, Amplification, and Case Studies
blurb: This paper analyzes the security of self-evolving LLM agent systems that autonomously update their parameters, memory, tools, and architectures, using a Module-Lifecycle Attack Surface matrix that finds 17 of 25 cells face critical threats with no effective mitigation. Case studies of two open-source frameworks show evolution-native design activates 3.5x more attack surface and achieves 100% attack persistence across 40 payloads, while co-located security scanners block only 2.5% of attacks.

title: DE-FIVE: Detecting Malicious Image Prompts via Fourier Features and Image Vector Embeddings
blurb: DE-FIVE is a training-free framework for detecting malicious image prompts against vision language models, combining a black-box detector that operates on Fourier-domain features with a white-box detector that exploits visual encoder embeddings from a few-shot malicious set. The authors target indirect prompt injection, an underdefended attack surface, and report that the hybrid approach outperforms state-of-the-art baselines without retraining or extra classifiers.

title: RAVEN: Agentic RAG for Automated Vulnerability Repair
blurb: RAVEN is an agentic retrieval-augmented generation framework for automated vulnerability repair that runs on locally deployable open-source LLMs with limited GPU requirements. It pairs a multi-faceted retrieval pipeline for historical fixes with a Curator Agent that pulls cross-file dependencies, achieving an 83.13% repair success rate across 160 real-world CVEs spanning two languages and unseen CWE categories.

title: The Table Says Otherwise: Testing LLMs with Counterfactual Relational Data
blurb: This paper introduces ContraTable, a paired original-counterfactual benchmark that tests whether LLMs ground table-QA answers in the provided relational data or fall back on facts memorized during pretraining. Across 214 matched questions spanning single-table, multi-table, and temporal reasoning, models handle direct lookups well but grow less reliable on joins, comparisons, and temporal reasoning, with the original-counterfactual accuracy gap exposing where they default to prior knowledge over table evidence.

title: AIR: Adaptive Interleaved Reasoning with Code in MLLMs
blurb: This paper introduces AIR, a method that gives multimodal LLMs adaptive interleaved reasoning by training them with reinforcement learning on code-augmented numerical computation tasks, going beyond prior tool-use work limited to visual perception. Using a group-constrained reward function, AIR improves benchmark performance by an average of 6.1 percentage points, raises interleaved reasoning accuracy by 9.9 points, and pushes tool-use success above 95%.

title: OpenAI rolls out AI-led push to fix open-source software flaws
blurb: OpenAI launched Patch the Planet, a program with cybersecurity firm Trail of Bits that uses AI-assisted research and human review to find and fix vulnerabilities in widely used open-source projects including Python, Go, cURL, and pyca/cryptography. The company said the work has already identified hundreds of security issues and merged dozens of patches, with Trail of Bits engineers filtering false positives before findings reach maintainers.

title: Muown Implicitly Performs Angular Step-size Decay
blurb: This paper shows that Muown's directional update is equivalent to a Riemannian step on normalized weight directions, with the un-normalized magnitude only modulating the angular step size. The authors use this to build AngularMuown, which makes the angular step size explicit and schedulable, improves over Muown, and currently leads the per-optimizer category of the modded nanoGPT speedrunning competition.

title: AutoDex: An Automated Real-World System for Dexterous Grasping Data Collection
blurb: AutoDex is an automated system that collects real-world dexterous grasping data by closing the full perception, execution, labeling, and reset loop without human intervention. It gathered 3,593 grasp trials across Allegro and Inspire hands on 100 objects, ran 4.8x faster than teleoperation, and produced a validated database whose retrieved grasps succeed 76% of the time versus 34% for simulation-only validation.

title: Managing Procedural Memory in LLM Agents: Control, Adaptation, and Evaluation
blurb: This paper introduces AFTER, a benchmark of 382 enterprise tasks across six professional roles and 22 procedural skills, designed to test how LLM agents reuse learned procedures across tasks, roles, and model backbones. A single refinement round raised aggregate performance by 3.7 to 6.7 points, and skills evolved from multi-model execution traces hit 73.1% cross-model accuracy, outperforming any single-model trace source.

title: A Watermark for Vision-Language-Action and World Action Models
blurb: This paper proposes keyed latent-provenance verification, a watermarking method that fingerprints vision-language-action and world-action robot policies by swapping the Gaussian noise seed for a keyed one with the same distribution, leaving the model's actions statistically indistinguishable from an ordinary run. Tested on two models across two robot suites, the fingerprint stays detectable under output-side removal attacks and weight-level edits with little change to task performance.

title: MacAgentBench: Benchmarking AI Agents on Real-World macOS Desktop
blurb: MacAgentBench is a macOS agent benchmark of 676 tasks across 25 applications, nearly 60% of which require both GUI and CLI interaction, using deterministic rule-based evaluation with fine-grained multi-checkpoint scoring. Across three frameworks and 16 models, Claude Opus 4.6 on OpenClaw scored the best 73.7% Pass@1, an advantage the authors attribute to the skill library rather than framework design.

title: AgentLens: Interpretable Safety Steering via Mechanistic Subspaces for Multi-Turn Coding Agent
blurb: AgentLens is a white-box defense framework that detects harmful execution states in multi-turn coding agents from step-level hidden representations and mitigates unsafe behavior by intervening in a 10-dimensional subspace within a single layer. The authors also release the Mechanistic Agent Safety benchmark, with annotated multi-turn trajectories across 194 tasks on LLaMA-3.1-8B, Qwen-2.5-7B, and Gemma-2-9B, showing strong detection and substantially fewer harmful actions.

title: Solve for the Hyperparameter, Skip the Search: Kolmogorov-Optimal Scaling Laws for Spline Regression
blurb: This paper introduces KORE (Kolmogorov-optimal Order-aware Resolution Estimation), a method that solves for the optimal spline regression resolution in closed form instead of searching a hyperparameter grid. Across additive and sparse pairwise targets up to 80 dimensions it matches exhaustive cross-validation while fitting roughly 8x fewer models, and ranks first among 21 methods for accuracy per unit of compute on 36 real tabular datasets.

title: VeriEvol: Scaling Multimodal Mathematical Reasoning via Verifiable Evol-Instruct
blurb: VeriEvol is an iterative framework that scales visual mathematical reasoning data by separating prompt difficulty, expanded through type-aware evolution operators, from answer reliability, enforced by a HTV-Agent verifier that accepts answers only after multi-source counter-evidence fails to refute them. Scaling evolved SFT data from 10K to 250K samples lifted mean accuracy from 35.42 to 54.73, and the verified pipeline added 3.88 points over an un-evolved RL baseline.

title: Getty Just Became an AI Company. It Now Has to Perform Like One
blurb: Getty Images stock soared 145% after the company signed a content licensing deal with OpenAI, repositioning the stock-image giant around AI. Getty had pursued home-grown AI tools before the partnership, and the surge now leaves it under pressure to deliver on the AI-company valuation the deal implies.

title: UnBias-Plus: Detect, Explain, and Rewrite Bias
blurb: UnBias-Plus is an open-source toolkit that unifies segment-level bias classification, biased span localization, neutral text rewriting, and per-decision reasoning into a single pipeline for human-written and AI-generated text. It ships with trained models, datasets, and documentation, and is accessible through Python, CLI, REST API, and web interfaces.

title: Scaling LLM Knowledge Boundaries via Distribution-Optimized Synthesis
blurb: This paper introduces KDoS (Knowledge Distribution-optimized Synthesis), a framework that uses knowledge density to guide synthetic data generation through a three-stage feedback loop rather than stopping at preset token counts or fixed ratios. Testing on models from 0.6B to 16B parameters across Qwen, Ling, and LLaMA, the authors find an optimal knowledge distribution that consistently maximizes knowledge boundary expansion and outperforms baselines on six benchmarks.

title: SelPE: Progressive Selection for Private Structured Text Synthesis
blurb: SelPE is a differential privacy framework for synthesizing structured text records, such as clinical triage notes and financial logs, when only a few private examples are available. It concentrates the privacy budget on a sequence of multi-batch top-1 selections rather than noisy aggregation or private model training, decoupling semantic abstraction from schema realization to improve structural validity, fidelity, and downstream utility under tight budgets.

title: Open Problem: Is AdamW Effective Under Heavy-Tailed Noise?
blurb: This paper formulates as an open problem whether AdamW, the de facto optimizer for LLM training, can provably converge under the heavy-tailed gradient noise observed in pretraining, a regime where sign-based methods like Lion and Muon already have sharp rates. The authors prove a positive weighted-metric benchmark but also give a corridor lower-bound mechanism showing how AdamW's second-moment accumulator can hide large gradients.

title: Causal Discovery in the Era of Agents
blurb: This paper argues that LLM agents should assist causal discovery without supplying the causal claims. The authors contend that asking models to infer edge directions, propose graph structures, or inject priors risks passing off textual associations, prompt artifacts, and hallucinated mechanisms as evidence. They propose causal-learn+, a platform where agents handle data inspection, method recommendation, and interpretation while orientations and conclusions stay grounded in formal algorithms and expert decisions, demonstrated on Big Five personality data.

title: Uncertainty-based Debiasing and Unlearning for Decontamination
blurb: This paper introduces Uncertainty-Based Decontamination (UBD), a family of methods that estimates per-sample memorization from deep ensembles of a contaminated model without needing an uncontaminated reference or labels for which samples are contaminated. On MMLU-Pro and MATH-MCQA, UBD recovers output distributions closer to an uncontaminated model than paraphrasing or choice-permutation baselines while preserving accuracy on clean data.

title: Knowledge-Graph Grounding Helps LLMs Only for Out-of-Training Knowledge: A Controlled Study on Clinical Question Answering
blurb: This controlled study finds that knowledge-graph grounding improves LLM accuracy only when the decisive fact lies outside the model's training data. Using the PrimeKG biomedical graph, neither triple retrieval nor an agentic text-to-Cypher loop moved MedQA scores, but on synthetic counterfactual and novel-fact benchmarks the same pipeline lifted out-of-training accuracy from chance to roughly 100%.

title: Discovering Latent Groups for Robust Classification
blurb: This paper introduces neural classification trees (NCT), a framework that builds robustness to spurious correlations by encoding subgroup structure directly in a tree-shaped architecture. NCT routes each sample to an "easy" or "hard" node based on prediction correctness and reuses those routes as pseudo-labels across iterations, isolating minority subgroups without subgroup supervision while matching state-of-the-art robustness on five benchmarks.

title: Tapered Language Models
blurb: This paper challenges the convention that language models allocate parameters uniformly across layers, showing that under a fixed budget, giving earlier layers more capacity and later layers less improves perplexity. The authors introduce Tapered Language Models, which monotonically taper MLP width across depth via a cosine schedule, consistently improving perplexity and benchmark performance across three scales and four architectures at no extra parameter or compute cost.

title: Diffusion Models Adapt to Low-Dimensional Structure Under Flexible Coefficient Choices
blurb: This paper proves that diffusion models' ability to exploit unknown low-dimensional data structure holds across a broad class of update coefficients, not just the narrowly prescribed ones studied in prior convergence theory. The authors show that Õ(k/ε) iterations suffice to produce an ε-accurate sample in total variation distance, independent of ambient dimension, justifying why many practical samplers work well on structured high-dimensional data.

title: ReasoningLens: Hierarchical Visualization and Diagnostic Auditing for Large Reasoning Models
blurb: ReasoningLens is an open-source framework for hierarchically visualizing and auditing the long Chain-of-Thought traces produced by large reasoning models. It structures traces into interactive hierarchies that separate strategy from execution, uses an agentic auditor for automated error detection and tool-augmented verification, and synthesizes reasoning profiles that surface model-specific blind spots.

title: Scaling Linear Mode Connectivity and Merging to Billion Parameter Pretrained Transformers
blurb: This paper introduces a scalable framework for applying linear mode connectivity to merge independently trained billion-parameter pretrained transformers, using functionality-preserving weight transformations and a dual learning procedure where both models jointly align toward a shared interpolation path. The method achieves near-zero loss barriers on WikiText for medium-sized language models, keeps ViT-L above 69% ImageNet top-1 accuracy across the interpolation path, and leaves only small barriers for billion-parameter LLMs.

title: EnerInfer: Energy-Aware On-Device LLM Inference
blurb: EnerInfer is an on-device LLM inference framework that jointly manages energy efficiency, throughput, and thermal comfort by modestly lowering NPU and memory frequencies where configuration slack exists. It predicts power and throughput for unseen models across frequency settings and uses limited-horizon thermal prediction, improving energy efficiency by up to 65% on phones, 12% on a laptop, and 24% on a development board without QoE violations.

title: SemCEB: A Cardinality Estimation Benchmark for Semantic Operators
blurb: SemCEB is the first benchmark for cardinality estimation over semantic operators, the LLM-defined SQL filters and joins whose high per-tuple costs make bad query plans orders of magnitude more expensive. Built on a real-world dataset with 102 hand-curated queries, it evaluates sampling-based methods and Semantic Histograms, finding that sampling is robust across predicate categories but scales poorly and costs too much.

title: Approximating velocity fields with planted attractors via Neural-ODEs for classification purposes
blurb: This paper uses Neural ODEs with a curated set of planted equilibrium points to perform classification, assigning each target class its own attractor in the learned velocity field. Inputs are treated as initial conditions whose trajectories flow into the basin of attraction matching their class, with the network's universal approximation capacity shaping the dynamics that route each point to its destination.

title: MAS-PromptBench: When Does Prompt Optimization Improve Multi-Agent LLM Systems?
blurb: MAS-PromptBench is a benchmark that studies when optimizing system prompts improves multi-agent LLM systems, which face an exponentially larger search space than single-agent setups. The authors test two prompt optimizers extended from single-agent methods across varying tasks, workflows, communication protocols, and team sizes, characterizing where the gains are real and where they break down.

title: [AINews] SpaceX is already a $28B/yr Neocloud
blurb: SpaceX's GPU rental business now annualizes to roughly $28B a year, according to Clouded Judgement's Jamin Ball, after a third compute deal with Reflection AI added to existing agreements with Anthropic and Google. The estimate works out to $2.32B per month at over $10 per hour for Blackwell GPUs, roughly twice CoreWeave's current revenue.

title: TriggerBench: Investigating Prospective Memory for Large Language Models
blurb: TriggerBench is a benchmark for evaluating prospective memory in LLMs, the ability to spontaneously recall and act on latent constraints without being explicitly prompted, across five dimensions spanning daily assistant and professional workflows. The authors find that prospective memory is much harder than retrospective memory, decaying sharply as context length scales while retrospective recall stays near-saturated up to 100K tokens, and that it tracks a model's spare reasoning budget.

title: Learning Process Rewards via Success Visitation Matching for Efficient RL
blurb: This paper introduces a method that converts sparse outcome rewards into dense process rewards by training a discriminator to separate successful from unsuccessful episodes, then rewarding the policy for matching the state-action visitations of successful runs. The authors show it provably preserves the optimal policy and significantly speeds up RL finetuning of robotic control on both simulated and real-world manipulation tasks.

title: On the Limits of Prompt-Conditioned Language Models as General-Purpose Learners
blurb: This paper argues that LLMs are not universal problem solvers through prompting alone. Modeling user-system interaction as a cheap-talk game, the authors derive PAC-Bayes bounds and identify two irreducible error floors: an expressivity floor, where language's limited channel capacity makes complex tasks indistinguishable, and an objective-misalignment floor, where alignment constraints push the ideal output outside the feasible set. They suggest multimodal inputs and external memory could lift these limits.

title: HADES: Privacy-Preserving Federated Learning via Selective Feature Encryption and Hybrid Model Fusion
blurb: HADES is a federated learning framework that uses PCA to identify the most privacy-sensitive features and encrypts only those via multiparty homomorphic encryption, while training a plaintext network on the remaining features and merging the two through a fusion mechanism. The selective approach reduces reconstruction attack success and matches vanilla FL accuracy while cutting the runtime overhead of fully encrypted training pipelines.

title: CFPO: Counterfactual Policy Optimization for Multimodal Reasoning
blurb: CFPO (Counterfactual Policy Optimization) is a reinforcement learning framework for large vision-language models that enforces causal consistency between visual perception and textual reasoning, addressing grounding failures where models ignore visual evidence in favor of language priors. It regularizes the policy by maximizing the discrepancy against a counterfactual state with critical visual cues suppressed, integrating with GRPO and DAPO without external reward models and beating standard RL baselines by 3.17% to 6.25%.

title: From Fragments to Paths: Task-Level Context Recovery for Large Industrial Codebases
blurb: DeepDiscovery is a task-level repository-understanding method that uses a two-stage Location–Inference framework to localize high-confidence task anchors and recover broader task-relevant context across multi-relational codebase structure under budget constraints. On SWE-bench Verified it reaches a 78.6% solve rate, beating its baseline by 8.2 percentage points, with file recall gains of 1.6 to 9.2 points on large industrial subprojects.

title: SK hynix passes Samsung as South Korea's most valuable company — memory company surpasses valuation milestone on the back of HBM
blurb: SK hynix overtook Samsung Electronics on Monday to become South Korea's most valuable listed company, the first time Samsung has lost the top spot on the KOSPI since November 2000, after a 340% rally this year built on HBM demand. The pure-play memory maker held 61% of the global HBM market in 2025 against Samsung's 17%, with Samsung's HBM3E yield delays slowing its Nvidia orders.

title: Teaching LLMs String Matching, Backtracking, and Error Recovery to Deduce Bases and Truth Tables for the Combinatorially Exploding Bit Manipulation Puzzles
blurb: This paper presents an approach to the NVIDIA Nemotron Model Reasoning Challenge's Bit Manipulation Puzzles, where LLMs must infer a hidden logical rule mapping binary inputs to outputs. Rather than simulating boolean arithmetic, the authors reframe logic-gate deduction as base selection using string similarity, add backtracking DFS with error recovery, and tokenize binary strings as single-bit tokens, reaching over 96% validation accuracy for a 7th-place overall finish.

title: It's Much Easier for Neural Networks to learn Game of Life Dynamics with the Right Activation Function: Polynomial Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks
blurb: This paper revisits whether neural networks need scale to learn Conway's Game of Life, finding that the right activation function matters more than network size. A 2nd-degree polynomial activation reliably learns Life dynamics in minimal networks, even without weight training, while standard ReLU networks depend on large size and lucky initializations to succeed.

title: AI Exposure Scores: what they measure, what they miss, and what comes next
blurb: This paper revisits the influential "GPTs are GPTs" exposure scores from Eloundou et al. (2023), which define AI exposure as the share of occupational tasks an LLM can assist with. The authors argue these static scores have two widening gaps: a structural one between what they measure and what policy questions require, and a coordination gap where policymakers keep citing the original scores while ignoring methodological updates. They survey five families of newer measures and call for participatory methods, better data infrastructure, and a shift from prediction to preparedness.

title: A Spectral Theory of Normalized Corrected GNN Propagation
blurb: This paper develops a spectral theory for normalized corrected GNN propagation, studying the symmetric normalized adjacency with its degree-stationary component removed to isolate the direction tied to oversmoothing. The main result is a high-probability exact-recovery theorem for the binary Contextual Stochastic Block Model after O(log n) propagation steps, with a multi-class partial recovery result and synthetic and real node-classification experiments confirming the predicted dependence on depth, graph signal, and feature noise.

title: Data Selection Through Iterative Self-Filtering for Vision-Language Settings
blurb: This paper introduces Self-Filtering, a bootstrapped method that trains a CLIP model on an evolving, self-selected dataset rather than relying on heuristics, curated reference sets, or pre-trained models. The dataset iterates between training and reselection, balancing highly probable clean samples with diverse samples from the full distribution to improve downstream performance without additional data.

title: Towards Root Memories: Benchmarking and Enhancing Implicit Logical Memory Retrieval for Personalized LLMs
blurb: This paper introduces IMLogic, a benchmark for implicit logical memory retrieval in long-dialogue settings, where personalized LLMs often miss logically critical memories that share little semantic overlap with the query. The authors also propose RootMem, a plug-and-play framework that distills user histories into structured "root memories" and uses an LLM-based router to activate logically relevant ones, outperforming the strongest retrieval baselines and improving existing memory agents.

title: Text2DSL: LLM-Based Code Generation for Domain-Specific Languages
blurb: This paper formalizes Text2DSL, the task of generating domain-specific language code from natural language descriptions, as a problem class distinct from Text-to-SQL and general-purpose code generation. The authors release PolkitBench, 4,204 verified natural-language-to-Polkit-rule pairs, and show that supplying formal context such as BNF grammar and API specifications raises syntactic validity to 98.6-99.4% and CodeBLEU scores by 60% to 95% without fine-tuning.

title: Quantifying the Agreement Between Data-Influence and Data-Similarity to Understand LLM Behavior
blurb: This paper compares two methods for tracing LLM outputs back to training data: cheaper data-similarity and more accurate data-influence. The authors rank training documents by each measure and find the two agree significantly but asymmetrically, with data-similarity's top documents getting more consistent influence ranks than the reverse, a pattern that holds across OLMo2-1B, Qwen3-1.7B, Llama3.2-1B, Gemma3-1B, and GPT2 and enables a favorable cost-accuracy trade-off.

title: Building a code reviewer from your team's PR history
blurb: This post describes how an engineer built an AI code reviewer that mines three years of a team's PR comments and writes feedback in each reviewer's individual style. The system encodes each engineer's known preferences, such as one reviewer's veto on MobX dependencies and another's scrutiny of database queries per loop, applying the whole team's standards to every PR.

title: RECALL: Recovery Experience Collection for Active Lifelong Learning in Vision-Language-Action Models
blurb: This paper proposes an active, continual learning approach for fine-tuning Vision-Language-Action models, replacing passive imitation learning with uncertainty-guided collection of recovery demonstrations. The authors show this improves adaptation efficiency but causes catastrophic forgetting, and they evaluate replay-based data mixing and elastic weight consolidation to balance plasticity against retention of previously learned behaviors.

title: WaveDetect: Robust Framework for Machine-Generated Text Detection via Wavelet Transform
blurb: WaveDetect is a framework that reframes machine-generated text detection as a signal processing problem, modeling token probabilities as a signal and applying a differentiable Continuous Wavelet Transform to extract spectral fingerprints invisible in the time domain. It reaches state-of-the-art accuracy on the RAID, EvoBench, and Domain-Shift datasets, holding up against adversarial perturbations, cross-domain shifts, and unseen evolving LLMs.

title: RigorBench: Benchmarking Engineering Process Discipline in Autonomous AI Coding Agents
blurb: RigorBench is an open-source benchmark that measures the engineering process discipline of autonomous coding agents rather than just whether their output passes tests. It scores harnesses across five pillars including planning fidelity, verification coverage, and recovery efficiency, finding that structured discipline raised process quality by an average of 41% and downstream outcome correctness by 17% across 30 tasks.

title: When Does Intrinsic Self-Correction Help? A Task-Sensitive Analysis
blurb: This paper takes a task-sensitive view of intrinsic self-correction, where a model revises its own answer without external feedback, examining when revision actually helps rather than whether it works in general. Across multiple benchmarks and models, the authors find consistent gains when task structure supports the revision mode, such as verifying explicit constraints or offering a second opinion in word games, framing self-correction as a task-dependent inference-time strategy.

title: Public Diffusion Models, Private Images: Key-Controlled Inversion for Conditional Reconstruction
blurb: This paper presents a key-controlled inversion framework that protects images processed by publicly available, white-box diffusion models, where anyone holding an intermediate latent can otherwise reconstruct the original input. By injecting key-dependent noise into the inversion formula, only users with the correct key can recover the image, and the authors prove the resulting ciphertext is IND-CPA secure with an adversary advantage that is exponentially small.

title: Hallucinations in Organization-backed AI advisors: Evidence about Skepticism, Verification, and Reliance in Goal-Directed Use
blurb: This paper reviews empirical evidence on how users handle hallucinations from organization-backed AI advisors, arguing that studies routinely conflate four distinct things: whether users are skeptical, whether they verify, whether verification succeeds, and whether it changes reliance. Across product search, medical decisions, and chatbot tasks, nearly all work measures reliance but rarely measures skepticism or verification directly. The most deployable intervention for organizations, generic hallucination warnings, shows the weakest and most mixed effects.

title: Semantic Browsing: Controllable Diversity for Image Generation
blurb: This paper introduces a method for controlled diversity in text-to-image generation called Semantic Browsing, which lets users navigate structured image galleries along interpretable axes of variation rather than incidental noise. The approach induces diversity at the text level, using an agentic VLM workflow to enforce structured variation attuned to the original prompt, so each output corresponds to a specific, user-understandable semantic decision.

title: SQLConductor: Search-to-Policy Learning for Step-wise Text-to-SQL Orchestration
blurb: SQLConductor is a step-wise orchestration framework for Text-to-SQL that trains a policy model to pick the next subtask action based on intermediate artifacts and feedback, rather than committing to a fixed pipeline upfront. The policy is learned via Search-to-Policy Learning, using Monte Carlo Tree Search to explore workflows and stability-weighted fine-tuning plus curriculum reinforcement learning, reaching 73.2% execution accuracy on BIRD-Dev while coordinating frozen larger action models.

title: The Energy Consumption of Transformer Fine-Tuning: A Roofline-Inspired Scaling Model
blurb: This paper introduces a framework for predicting the energy consumption of Transformer training across multiple GPUs, relating measured energy to lightweight proxies for compute, memory traffic, and hardware efficiency. Using controlled architectural sweeps of BERT models, the authors derive a roofline-inspired scaling law that incorporates a speedup-based efficiency factor for tensor and fully sharded data parallelism, accurately predicting training energy across heterogeneous configurations.

title: MuPPET: A Benchmark for Contextual Privacy of LLM Assistants in Multi-Party Conversations
blurb: MuPPET is a benchmark for testing contextual privacy of LLM assistants in multi-party conversations like group chats, where any disclosure reaches every participant at once. Experiments show models leak substantially more in multi-party settings than one-to-one evaluations suggest, with smaller open-weights models faring worst and existing privacy defenses offering only partial protection while degrading utility.

title: Dynamic estimation of slowly varying sequences
blurb: This paper introduces a framework for sequentially estimating functions over slowly-varying sequences, where consecutive elements differ by a small magnitude, generalizing implicit trace estimation to matrix powers, spectral densities, Monte Carlo integration, and PDE boundary value problems. Its adaptive algorithm scales the estimation budget locally with each step's variation, achieving path-length cost bounds of O(Σαᵢ) that improve on the prior O(m·maxαᵢ) worst-case guarantee for stable sequences with rare bursts.

title: VCT: A Verifiable Transcript System for LLM Conversations
blurb: This paper introduces VCT, a Verifiable Conversation Transcript system that cryptographically logs LLM interactions for forensics and compliance, capturing non-linear behaviors like re-prompting, response regeneration, deletion, and multi-device concurrency that linear tamper-evident logs miss. VCT uses a three-tier structure of branch-level hash chains, session-level Merkle roots, and an account-level root signed jointly by user and server, adding only 0.9% storage overhead at sub-millisecond to low-millisecond latency.

title: VADAOrchestra: Neurosymbolic Orchestration of Adaptive Reasoning Workflows
blurb: VADAOrchestra is a neurosymbolic framework that pairs an LLM orchestrator with a Datalog+/- symbolic engine, letting the LLM incrementally plan workflows as logic programs while all inference runs on the symbolic side. The split produces a verifiable reasoning trace for auditability and scales to large datasets through targeted querying, which the authors validate on real-world financial use cases against standard agentic architectures.

title: Towards Robust Personalized Federated Learning: Vulnerability Assessment and Defense Co-Design
blurb: This paper presents the first systematic study of adversarial threats in personalized federated learning (PFL), showing that PFL methods are more vulnerable to transfer-based adversarial attacks than centralized learning, since malicious clients can use local model knowledge to craft adversarial examples that compromise peers' personalized models. The authors propose a defense combining stochastic input noise, input-scaled trace regularization, and parameter sensitivity maximization, validated theoretically and across multiple benchmark datasets.

title: TailorMind: Towards Preference-Aligned Multimodal Content Generation
blurb: TailorMind generates user-tailored multimodal content without existing item pools, combining hypergraph collaborative filtering with controllable generation for up to 29% recall gains.

title: Anthropic prepares Cowork support for mobile apps
blurb: Anthropic is preparing to extend Cowork, its agentic system for knowledge work, to mobile, with a feature-flagged entry in an iOS build that lets users schedule tasks from a phone and track them across web, mobile, or desktop. The new copy points to moving execution to the cloud rather than running locally, lifting the constraint of March's Dispatch feature that required keeping a desktop awake.

title: UK government backs university AI labs with £60M to make AI cheaper
blurb: The UK government committed up to £60M to new AI research labs at Oxford and University College London, the first major investment under UKRI's national AI strategy. The labs aim to reduce reliance on US providers, with UCL building open-source models that run on widely available hardware and Oxford pursuing AI methods that avoid vast centralized compute.

title: AI-driven Optimisation of Quality of Recovery (QoR) in Remote Patient Monitoring
blurb: This paper introduces QoR-compact, a five-item daily survey that matches the full QoR-15 in predicting postoperative recovery severity (AUC-ROC 0.968).

title: The man who built Groq handed its technology to NVIDIA, now his old company raised $650M to fight back without him
blurb: Groq raised $650M to scale its LPU inference chips and compete with NVIDIA in the AI hardware market. The round comes as founder Jonathan Ross, who co-developed Google's tensor processing unit before starting the company, steps back, leaving Groq to challenge NVIDIA's dominance on inference economics without the engineer who defined its architecture.

title: Human and AI collaboration for pulmonary nodule segmentation
blurb: Hi-Seg, a human-in-the-loop framework built on SAM, hit ~85% Dice on pulmonary nodule segmentation across 1,179 patients, beating prior models by 10-22%.

title: Memory Chips and China, Microsoft and Chinese Models
blurb: The big three memory makers may come to regret letting Chinese rivals through the door, the same pattern that hollowed out incumbents in solar and displays once domestic capacity scaled. Microsoft, meanwhile, has every incentive to run Chinese open models, which are now close enough on quality and far cheaper to serve at the scale of its own products. The throughline is that China keeps moving up the stack, and the Western firms supplying or competing with it are starting to feed the thing that undercuts them.

title: Getty Just Became an AI Company. It Now Has to Perform Like One
blurb: Getty Images stock soared 145% after signing a content-licensing deal with OpenAI, repricing the company overnight from a stock-image library into an AI data supplier. Getty spent years building its own AI image tools and got little traction for the effort, so the market is now valuing it on someone else's model rather than its own. The deal turns Getty's archive into raw material for the labs, and the harder part is proving that licensing revenue can compound the way investors are suddenly betting it will.

title: Oracle Sheds 21,000 Jobs as It Sharpens Focus on AI
blurb: Oracle cut about 21,000 jobs last fiscal year, shrinking its head count roughly 13% even as it poured money into AI infrastructure. The layoffs land while the company commits to massive data-center buildouts to serve cloud and AI demand, including the compute behind its OpenAI deal. The pattern is now familiar across big tech: trim the workforce, redirect the savings into capex, and bet the growth comes from AI capacity rather than people.

title: Oracle sheds 21,000 roles over the past year amid wave of AI layoffs from tech giants
blurb: Oracle cut 21,000 jobs over the past year, almost 13% of its workforce, dropping from 162,000 to 141,000 employees and citing AI adoption directly in its annual filing. The cuts cost $1.8B in restructuring, nearly five times the prior year, even as capex jumped 162% to $55.7B and free cash flow ran to negative $23.7B. Oracle joins Meta, Microsoft, Google, and Amazon trading headcount for AI infrastructure spend that could hit $700B this year, a sign the labs and hyperscalers now treat compute, not people, as the scarce input.

title: They sold their startup to Datadog. Now, they’ve raised $35M to disrupt it
blurb: Two former Datadog engineers who sold their last startup to Datadog raised $35M in Series A to undercut it, led by Singular with General Catalyst, Databricks Ventures, and DST Global Partners joining. Their company, Tsuga, runs observability inside the customer's own cloud instead of shipping telemetry to a vendor, avoiding the per-byte fees that make legacy platforms unaffordable for AI workloads. Six months after launch it has several million in revenue on six-figure average contracts, with Black Forest Labs and Le Monde as customers, in a market growing from $4.1B to a projected $18.1B by 2034.

title: [AINews] SpaceX is already a $28B/yr Neocloud
blurb: SpaceX just signed its third GPU rental deal, this one with Reflection AI, on top of its existing Anthropic and Google contracts, quietly turning the rocket company into one of the largest neoclouds in the market. Jamin Ball tallied the run rate at $2.32B per month, above $10 an hour for Blackwells, which annualizes to about $28B a year. That is roughly twice CoreWeave's current revenue, and CoreWeave still commands a $60B valuation a year after its IPO.
