The compute buildout is now a balance-sheet game, and the biggest players selling capacity are not the ones you would expect.

title: Mach Industries raised $300M at a $1.8B valuation — and its new in-house rocket-motor arm targets a U.S. supply bottleneck
blurb: Mach Industries raised $300M at a $1.8B valuation, roughly quadrupling its paper value in a year, but the more telling move came two weeks earlier when it acquired propulsion firm Exquadrum and folded it in as Mach Energetics. The U.S. has only a handful of solid rocket motor suppliers, the components that propel most missiles and munitions a drone-heavy force expects to fire in quantity. A 2023-founded startup buying its way into that bottleneck is a sharper bet than the valuation suggests: it is moving to own the scarcest link in its own supply chain.

title: Habituation at the Gate: Rising Approval and Declining Scrutiny in Human Review of AI Agent Code
blurb: A longitudinal study of 400 repeat reviewers and 11,429 reviews over seven months found that human approval of AI-agent pull requests rose from 30.1% to 36.8% as reviewers gained experience, an effect that persists after controlling for calendar time and PR size. The authors read it as reflexive habituation rather than calibrated trust, since review latency grew 3.5x while inline comment volume fell 22%, meaning reviewers sat longer in queue but inspected code less.

title: Self-Compacting Language Model Agents
blurb: SelfCompact is an inference-time scaffold that lets an agent decide when to summarize its own context, pairing a compaction tool with a rubric that fires when a sub-task resolves or the trajectory converges and suppresses mid-derivation or when stuck. Across six benchmarks and seven models it matches or beats fixed-interval summarization with no fine-tuning, improving over no-summarization by up to 18.1 points on math at 30-70% lower per-question cost.

title: Kamera: Unified Position-Invariant Multimodal KV Cache for Training-Free Reuse
blurb: Kamera is a training-free KV cache scheme that lets multimodal agents reuse encoded video frames, screenshots, and rendered artifacts at any context position instead of re-encoding them on every look-back. It pairs exact RoPE re-rotation with a small low-rank conditioning patch that restores the cross-chunk binding naive reuse drops, recovering the multi-hop reasoning accuracy that blind position-independent caches halve while reconstructing re-prefill KV to bf16 rounding across six SGLang backbones.

title: Malicious npm Packages Pose as PostCSS Tools to Deliver Windows RAT
blurb: JFrog researchers flagged three malicious npm packages published by the user "abdrizak" that impersonate the widely used postcss-selector-parser library to drop a Windows remote access trojan. The packages run a JavaScript dropper that fetches a PowerShell downloader and a Nuitka-compiled Python RAT capable of stealing Chrome credentials, running shell commands, and exfiltrating files, and all three remain live on npm.

title: Understanding the (In)Security of Vibe-Coded Applications
blurb: This paper studies the security of vibe-coded applications built largely through natural-language prompting of AI agents, finding recurring flaws like placeholder logic, unfiltered input, and exposed secrets that differ from conventional codebases. The authors trace these to systematic agent limitations such as memory loss and weak security knowledge, and show that better models and prompting reduce but never eliminate the underlying risk.

title: Beyond Simpson's Paradox: A Cascade of Confounders in AI Agent Pull-Request Co-Authorship
blurb: The authors analyze 33,596 pull requests from five AI coding agents and show that the apparent co-authorship merge gap (53.8% vs 79.8% pooled) is a Simpson's Paradox driven by Codex dominating 64.9% of the data. After controlling for repository selection and PR structure like commit count, no agent retains a clear co-authorship effect, exposing the pooled statistic as a selection and structure artifact rather than causal evidence.

title: GroundEval: A Deterministic Replacement for LLM-as-Judge in Stateful Agent Evaluation
blurb: GroundEval is a judge-free framework that scores stateful agents against grounded, time-bounded, access-controlled evidence, checking the recorded trajectory of what was searched, fetched, and cited rather than just the final answer. In one case study, two frontier LLM judges rated a plausible response above 0.85, but GroundEval scored it 0.000 because the agent never retrieved the artifact its answer depended on.

title: An Introduction to YOLO26
blurb: Roboflow published a walkthrough of YOLO26, an end-to-end multi-task vision model family spanning detection, instance segmentation, pose estimation, oriented detection, and classification across five sizes from Nano to Extra Large. It removes Non-Maximum Suppression and drops the Distribution Focal Loss module to cut latency and improve edge and low-power hardware compatibility, with COCO benchmarks and comparisons against RF-DETR, LW-DETR, and D-FINE.

title: Sniff out stale AI override advice with this open source CLI
blurb: A new open source CLI scans AI override files like CLAUDE.md, .cursorrules, and AGENTS.md to flag guidance that has drifted out of sync with the codebase it points at. The tool targets a common failure mode where outdated instructions silently steer coding agents toward dead files, renamed functions, or conventions the repo no longer follows.

title: The new HTTP QUERY method explained
blurb: RFC 10008 introduces the HTTP QUERY method, a safe, idempotent verb that carries filter criteria in a request body instead of cramming complex relational queries, deep nesting, and arrays into GET query strings that hit URL length limits and get logged by middleware. The post explains why a GET with a body fails in practice, since proxies and servers inconsistently reject, drop, or honor the body.

title: RAVEN: Agentic RAG for Automated Vulnerability Repair
blurb: RAVEN is an agentic RAG framework for automated vulnerability repair that runs entirely on locally deployable open-source LLMs, pairing a multi-faceted retrieval pipeline over historical fixes with a Curator Agent that pulls cross-file dependencies to patch vulnerabilities local context alone can't fix. Across 160 real-world CVEs spanning two languages, unseen CWE categories, and out-of-distribution settings, it hit an 83.13% repair success rate, beating prior state-of-the-art frameworks at negligible cost.

title: Shipping huggingface_hub every week with AI, open tools, and a human in the loop
blurb: Hugging Face moved huggingface_hub from a 4-to-6-week release cadence to weekly shipping via a single GitHub Actions workflow built entirely on open-source tools and open-weights models. Mechanical steps like version bumps, tagging, and downstream test branches run automatically, while a human stays in the loop only for release notes and announcements, where judgment matters and no vendor contract or closed model is required.

title: We got local models to triage the OpenClaw repo for FREE!*
blurb: The author runs local open-weight models like Gemma and Qwen inside an agent harness with structured outputs to triage incoming issues and PRs for the OpenClaw repo, classifying each into a fixed label set instead of using a BERT-style classifier. Running on a 128GB GB10 box gives near-instant per-issue notifications at the cost of electricity, versus batching through a paid API plan to stay within quota.

title: AgentLens: Interpretable Safety Steering via Mechanistic Subspaces for Multi-Turn Coding Agent
blurb: AgentLens is a white-box defense for multi-turn LLM coding agents that detects harmful execution states from step-level hidden representations and mitigates them by intervening in a 10-dimensional subspace within a single layer. The authors also release the Mechanistic Agent Safety benchmark, with annotated execution trajectories across 194 tasks on LLaMA-3.1-8B, Qwen-2.5-7B, and Gemma-2-9B, reporting reduced harmful actions and early evidence of lookahead risk anticipation.

title: On Good Authority: Release-Authority Measurement for Registry-Mediated Package Ecosystems
blurb: This paper introduces a predecessor-aware release-authority record that compares each package release against its immediate predecessor across publisher, repository, workflow, provenance, signing, and mediation evidence to flag public release-path discontinuities worth reviewing. Run over 43,100 predecessor comparisons across npm, PyPI, Maven Central, and RubyGems, transparent rules surfaced 204 policy-triggering discontinuities, though the authors note these are review cues only and show zero overlap with known malicious versions in their external alignment.

title: VeriPort: Automated and Verified Patch Backporting at Scale
blurb: VeriPort is an agentic system that backports a security patch to every affected version of a dependency, building a chain of evidence that each backport blocks exploitation and preserves behavior. It resolves 95.3% of 128 BackportBench tasks, beating Claude Code by 22.7 points, and a deployment across 169 high- and critical-severity CVEs produced over 5,000 verified patches while exposing 2,100 versions wrongly flagged as affected.

title: In praise of memcached
blurb: The author argues that Redis often enters a stack as a volatile cache but gets quietly treated as a persistent database, leaving ops teams to maintain it like a fragile pet once apps depend on data surviving restarts. The case for memcached is that its deliberately minimal feature set keeps a cache a cache, removing the temptation to lean on it for persistence the team never planned to guarantee.

title: When Is a Columnar Scan Bandwidth-Bound? A Decode-Throughput Law and Its Cross-Hardware Validation
blurb: This paper models when a columnar scan is bandwidth-bound versus compute-bound, showing that a decoder's value throughput is essentially independent of bit-width and that the achieved bandwidth fraction follows a one-parameter law with a compute-to-bandwidth ridge at b* = 8*beta/T_dec. Fitting one throughput value per strategy reproduced measured bandwidth fractions with median error 0.027 on x86/AVX2 and 0.003 on a held-out Apple M4/NEON machine.

title: From Fragments to Paths: Task-Level Context Recovery for Large Industrial Codebases
blurb: DeepDiscovery is a task-level repository-understanding method that uses a two-stage Location-Inference framework to localize high-confidence task anchors and recover broader task-relevant context across multi-relational codebase structure under budget constraints. On SWE-bench Verified it lifts an agent's solve rate to 78.6%, 8.2 points over baseline, with file-recovery gains of 1.6 to 9.2 points on large industrial subprojects and no offline preprocessing required.

title: Building a code reviewer from your team's PR history
blurb: A staff engineer built an AI code reviewer that mines three years of his team's PR comments to give feedback in each reviewer's voice, learning that one teammate vetoes MobX and another counts the DB queries a loop fires. The post traces the build from a v0 Claude Code skill that checked diffs against a markdown checklist to a system that encodes individual reviewers' taste on every PR, with the repo open-sourced.

title: A Set-Theoretic Approach to Detecting Logic Bugs in DBMS Inner Join Optimizations
blurb: This paper presents JoinEquiv, a metamorphic testing tool that detects logic bugs in DBMS inner join optimization by rewriting NATURAL/INNER JOIN queries into semantically equivalent forms using set-theoretic transformations and comparing results against the originals. Running these oracles across MySQL, TiDB, DuckDB, and Percona surfaced 29 previously unknown issues, 27 of which were officially confirmed as logical flaws in query optimizers and executors.

title: RigorBench: Benchmarking Engineering Process Discipline in Autonomous AI Coding Agents
blurb: RigorBench is the first benchmark to score AI coding agents on engineering process rather than outcome correctness alone, rating harnesses like Agent-Skills and Superpowers across five pillars: planning fidelity, verification coverage, recovery efficiency, abstention quality, and atomic transition integrity. Across 30 tasks, the authors found that enforcing structured process discipline raised process-quality scores by 41% and downstream correctness by 17%, with the benchmark, rubrics, and trajectory tools released as open source.

title: Will It Mythos?
blurb: A developer built a benchmark from bugs Anthropic's Mythos found to test whether other models can detect them blind.

title: Source-Free Detection and Impact Analysis of Compiler Optimization Problems in Mobile Applications
blurb: OptDetect is a source-free framework that detects compiler optimization problems directly from app binaries, using disassembly, chunk-level classification, and weighted scoring to flag native libraries built at O0/O1 instead of O2/O3. Across 21,972 libraries from 830 top Google Play apps, 30.5% used low optimization levels, and fixing them cut CPU instructions by a median of 20.5% to 32%.

title: The Table Says Otherwise: Testing LLMs with Counterfactual Relational Data
blurb: This paper introduces ContraTable, a benchmark that tests whether LLMs answer table questions from the provided data or from memorized facts. It pairs an original database with a counterfactual version that keeps the same schema but rewrites country, club, and player attributes across 214 matched questions. Models handle single-table lookups but degrade on joins, comparisons, and temporal reasoning, falling back on pretrained knowledge when the table conflicts with familiar facts. The takeaway: table-QA evaluation should measure faithfulness, not just accuracy.

title: SelPE: Progressive Selection for Private Structured Text Synthesis
blurb: SelPE is a framework for generating synthetic structured text, like clinical triage notes or transaction logs, under differential privacy when only a handful of real examples exist. Instead of noisy aggregation or private model training, it spends the privacy budget on a sequence of top-1 selections and splits generation into semantic abstraction and schema realization. The authors report better structural validity, fidelity, and downstream utility than tabular or text-only DP methods in low-data regimes.

title: SemCEB: A Cardinality Estimation Benchmark for Semantic Operators
blurb: SemCEB is the first benchmark for cardinality estimation over semantic operators, the LLM-backed SQL filters and joins whose predicates are written in natural language. It spans 102 hand-curated queries across a range of selectivities, testing filters and joins in isolation. The stakes are cost: per-tuple LLM calls make bad plans orders of magnitude more expensive. Sampling proved robust but slow and pricey, while Semantic Histograms ran cheaper but stayed sensitive to predicate type.

title: A Compositional Language for Property Graphs
blurb: This paper argues that the newly standardized graph query languages GQL and SQL/PGQ have a core flaw: they aren't compositional, and despite handling reachability and all first-order queries, they can't express everything in NLOGSPACE. The authors propose a fix built on regular path queries with variables and a graph-to-graph language called #Datalog that can construct new nodes, edges, and paths, then map it to concrete additions the standards committee could adopt.

title: When Is a Columnar Scan Bandwidth-Bound? A Decode-Throughput Law and Its Cross-Hardware Validation
blurb: This paper offers a one-parameter law for when a columnar scan is actually memory-bandwidth-bound rather than compute-bound. The key finding is that a decoder's value throughput is independent of bit-width, so the achieved bandwidth fraction follows f = min(1, T_dec·b/8β), with the compute-to-bandwidth ridge at b* = 8β/T_dec. Fitting one parameter per strategy reproduced measured bandwidth fractions within median error 0.027 on x86/AVX2 and 0.003 on a held-out Apple M4.

title: SQLConductor: Search-to-Policy Learning for Step-wise Text-to-SQL Orchestration
blurb: SQLConductor proposes step-wise orchestration for text-to-SQL, training a policy model to pick the next subtask action based on intermediate artifacts and feedback rather than committing to a fixed pipeline upfront. The authors use Monte Carlo Tree Search to explore candidate workflows, then distill the robust patterns into a deployable policy via stability-weighted fine-tuning and curriculum RL. A compact policy coordinating frozen larger models hits 73.2% execution accuracy on BIRD-Dev, beating systems that train bigger SQL backbones directly.

title: A Set-Theoretic Approach to Detecting Logic Bugs in DBMS Inner Join Optimizations
blurb: This paper introduces JoinEquiv, a metamorphic testing tool that catches logic bugs in INNER JOIN optimization by rewriting a query into semantically equivalent forms using set-theory transformations, then flagging any case where the results diverge. Running it against MySQL, TiDB, DuckDB, and Percona surfaced 29 previously unknown issues, 27 now officially confirmed. The takeaway for data teams: optimizer correctness, not just crashes or latency, is a live failure mode worth testing against.

title: Bastiaan Jongerius Architecten cloaks Longhouse Farm with black timber and metal
blurb: Bastiaan Jongerius Architecten saved a 1970s farmhouse outside Amsterdam from demolition, extending its timber frame and re-cladding it in blackened Accoya wood and black standing-seam metal. The retrofit pairs a low-maintenance, energy-upgraded shell with double-height voids and aligned longitudinal axes that expose the original roof structure and frame the surrounding polder and village church.

title: Apple’s New CEO Must Rebuild a Design Team That Lost Its Way
blurb: Bloomberg reports that incoming Apple CEO John Ternus inherits a design organization that has drifted since Jony Ive's departure, with no clear successor to set hardware and interface direction. The same briefing outlines Apple's 2027 roadmap, including a redesigned iPhone and updated AirPods, raising the stakes for whoever Ternus installs to lead design.

title: Detecting Malicious Agent Skills in the Wild using Attention
blurb: A new paper introduces Locate-and-Judge, a two-stage detector for malicious LLM agent skills, where injected commands hide among legitimate instructions and evade prompt-injection defenses. Deployed at marketplace scale, it surfaced dozens of live malicious skills, several disguised as benign functionality and many missed by SkillSpector and Cisco Skill Scanner.

title: Exposing the Illusion of Erasure in Knowledge Editing for LLMs
blurb: This paper shows that Knowledge Editing methods for LLMs don't actually erase target facts but merely suppress and redistribute them, leaving the original knowledge recoverable through adversarial elicitation and indirect prompting across diverse architectures. The authors find edited facts sit in narrow, perturbation-sensitive regions of the loss landscape, making KE inherently bypassable and unreliable for removing sensitive or unsafe content from deployed models.

title: Trump Signs Executive Order Accelerating Post-Quantum Cryptography Migration
blurb: Trump signed Executive Order 14409 directing OMB, NIST, NSA, DHS, and CISA to develop technical guidance and accelerate federal post-quantum cryptography migration, citing the "harvest now, decrypt later" threat. Agencies must inventory high-value systems and transition key establishment to PQC by December 31, 2030 and digital signatures by 2031, and federal contractors must use NIST-compliant algorithms by the end of 2030.

title: Malicious npm Packages Pose as PostCSS Tools to Deliver Windows RAT
blurb: Three malicious npm packages published by user "abdrizak" (aes-decode-runner-pro, postcss-minify-selector, postcss-minify-selector-parser) typosquat the popular postcss-selector-parser library to deliver a Windows RAT, JFrog found. A JavaScript dropper writes a PowerShell downloader that pulls a Nuitka-compiled Python RAT capable of stealing Chrome credentials and running shell commands; all three remain live on npm and should be blocked immediately.

title: Trump signs executive orders setting 2031 deadline for post-quantum migration
blurb: Trump signed executive orders requiring all federal high-value assets and systems to migrate to post-quantum cryptography by the end of 2031. The deadline gives agencies and their vendors a fixed timeline to inventory cryptographic dependencies and deploy NIST-standardized PQC algorithms ahead of "harvest now, decrypt later" threats.

title: TROPT: An Open Framework for Unifying and Advancing Discrete Text Optimization
blurb: TROPT is the first open-source framework that unifies discrete text-trigger optimization, the search for input sequences that steer a model toward an attacker-specified objective and the basis for most LLM jailbreaks. It ships with 30+ recipes built from 15+ optimizers (white-box to black-box) and 15+ losses, letting red teams compare techniques head-to-head and port attacks across domains like corpus-poisoning embedding models. The authors' large-scale experiments surface potent but underadopted jailbreak strategies defenders should expect to see weaponized.

title: Russian Initial Access Broker Behind FortiBleed Campaign
blurb: A Russian initial access broker is running FortiBleed, a credential-harvesting campaign that SOCRadar says has scoped over 430,000 internet-exposed FortiGate firewalls since at least February, using a custom Golang sniffer to passively capture authentication traffic across 24 protocols. The financially motivated actor has compromised an estimated 110 million credentials and is still actively sniffing 19,000 targets, so treat edge appliances with exposed SSH or VPN as compromised and rotate credentials.

title: Understanding the (In)Security of Vibe-Coded Applications
blurb: This paper systematically audited a large corpus of real-world vibe-coded applications, finding recurring vulnerability classes that diverge from conventional software, including placeholder logic, unfiltered input, and exposed secrets. The authors trace these flaws to systematic AI-agent limitations such as memory loss, locally optimized objectives, and weak security knowledge, and show that stronger models and better prompting reduce but do not eliminate the underlying risk.

title: Attacking the Trusted Imagination: Oracle-Level Integrity Attacks on Imagine-then-Act World Models
blurb: This paper shows that the "imagine-then-act" design in vision-language-action policies exposes the world model's latent future trajectory, not the reactive policy, as the attack surface, letting an L-infinity-bounded observation perturbation corrupt downstream oracles like safety gates and MPC planners. Untargeted corruption runs roughly 60x stronger than random noise yet is detectable at AUC 1.0, and an imagination-driven MPC drops from 0.70 to 0.05 task success at epsilon=0.01.

title: FlexServe: A Fast and Secure LLM Serving System for Mobile Devices with Flexible Resource Isolation
blurb: This paper introduces FlexServe, a TrustZone-based system for running on-device LLM inference securely without the heavy isolation overhead that normally cripples it. It decouples access permission from management permission, letting the normal-world OS allocate and reclaim secure memory and NPU resources it can't read, protecting model weights and user data from a compromised kernel. FlexServe reports 10.05X average TTFT speedup over a baseline strawman and 2.44X over an optimized one.

title: TooBad: Backdoor Diffusion Models with Ultra-Low Poison Rate and Imperceptible Trigger
blurb: TooBad, a new backdoor framework for diffusion models, achieves over 85% attack success rate at just a 0.5% poison rate, far below the 10% prior methods required on benchmarks like CIFAR-10. The authors show it reaches near-100% ASR within 3-5 injection epochs, evades current SOTA defenses, and preserves model utility, raising the bar for backdoor detection on generative pipelines.

title: OpenAI wants AI to fix vulnerabilities, not just find them
blurb: OpenAI expanded Daybreak, its cybersecurity initiative, and updated Codex Security to move beyond discovery into automated remediation, generating codebase-specific patches that human operators review before applying. Since the March preview, Codex Security has scanned over 30M commits across 30,000 codebases and auto-confirmed more than 500,000 fixes, and the updated plugin triages findings from scanners, advisories, and bug bounties, then exports results via SARIF and CodeQL.

title: Safety in Self-Evolving LLM Agent Systems: Threats, Amplification, and Case Studies
blurb: This paper analyzes the security of self-evolving LLM agent systems, which autonomously update their own parameters, memory, tools, and architecture. Using a Module-Lifecycle Attack Surface matrix, the authors find that 17 of 25 cells face critical threats with no effective mitigation, and that evolution-native frameworks hit a 100% attack persistence rate (40/40 payloads) while co-located scanners blocked just 2.5%. The core finding: self-evolution converts session-bounded attacks into lineage-persistent ones that self-amplify across generations, making static defenses structurally inadequate.

title: Sniff out stale AI override advice with this open source CLI
blurb: A new open source CLI scans the instruction files that steer AI coding assistants and flags override rules that have gone stale, surfacing guidance that now points models toward deprecated or insecure patterns. Teams leaning on agent rule files should run it in CI to catch drift before outdated advice quietly degrades generated code or reintroduces fixed vulnerabilities.

title: DE-FIVE: Detecting Malicious Image Prompts via Fourier Features and Image Vector Embeddings
blurb: This paper introduces DE-FIVE, a training-free framework for detecting malicious image prompts against vision language models, targeting adversarial perturbations and indirect prompt injection delivered through crafted images. It pairs a black-box detector operating on Fourier-domain features with a white-box detector that uses image vector embeddings from the visual encoder, derived from only a few-shot malicious set. The authors report it consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines without retraining or deploying additional classifiers.

title: One-Prompt Censorship Evasion via Generative Diffusion Models
blurb: This paper reframes censorship evasion as a semantic image-to-image editing task, introducing FlowPaint, a diffusion-based framework that reshapes censored network traffic into benign-looking patterns from a single natural-language prompt. The authors report it outperforms existing evasion tools against both rule-based middleboxes and learning-based traffic classifiers, with users switching between censorship paradigms by varying the instruction rather than hand-tuning mechanism-specific configs. For defenders, it signals that DPI and ML traffic classifiers face an adversary that generalizes across modalities without manual fitting.

title: OpenAI rolls out AI-led push to fix open-source software flaws
blurb: OpenAI launched Patch the Planet with Trail of Bits, using AI-assisted vulnerability research plus human review to find and remediate flaws in core open-source projects including Python, Go, cURL, and pyca/cryptography. The program has already flagged hundreds of issues and merged dozens of patches, with Trail of Bits engineers filtering false positives before they reach maintainers. Analysts caution it only shifts enterprise risk if treated as one input to a supply-chain program, not a replacement for one.

title: RAVEN: Agentic RAG for Automated Vulnerability Repair
blurb: RAVEN is an agentic RAG framework for automated vulnerability repair that pairs a multi-faceted retrieval pipeline over historical fixes with a Curator Agent that pulls cross-file dependencies to patch vulnerabilities local context alone can't resolve. It runs on open-source LLMs in a fully local, low-GPU setup and hit an 83.13% repair success rate across 160 real-world CVEs spanning two languages, unseen CWE categories, and out-of-distribution settings, beating prior state-of-the-art frameworks at negligible cost.

title: WhatsApp VBScript Campaign Uses Fake Documents to Install ManageEngine RMM Tool
blurb: Kaspersky disclosed an active campaign abusing compromised WhatsApp Web and Desktop accounts to spread obfuscated VBScript files disguised as financial documents like "Financial Reports.vbs," which trigger a multi-stage chain that installs legitimate RMM software for persistent remote access. Targeting spans Malaysia (the highest victim concentration), Brazil, India, Mexico, and several other countries, with the script also masquerading as Windows Update components to evade scrutiny.

title: SelPE: Progressive Selection for Private Structured Text Synthesis
blurb: SelPE concentrates differential-privacy budget on top-1 selections to synthesize structured text from few private records.

title: VeriPort: Automated and Verified Patch Backporting at Scale
blurb: VeriPort is an agentic system that automatically backports security patches to every affected version of an open-source dependency, building an evidence chain to confirm each patch blocks exploitation and preserves functionality. It resolved 95.3% of 128 BackportBench tasks, beating Claude Code by 22.7 points, and generated over 5,000 verified patches across 169 high- and critical-severity CVEs. In the process it flagged 2,100 versions wrongly listed as affected and 127 previously unidentified vulnerable versions, prompting upstream corrections to 23 advisories.

title: Understanding the Stealthy BGP Hijacking Risk in the ROV Era
blurb: This paper formalizes stealthy BGP hijacking, a hijacking variant enabled by partial Route Origin Validation deployment that diverts traffic along malicious routes without reaching victims, leaving them unalerted. Using the SHAMAN inference framework across 8.3 billion routes, the authors measured a 14.1% overall success probability, rising to 99.5% for targeted attacks against specific victim-target-hijacker tuples, with 95.9% incident-level validation accuracy.

title: On Good Authority: Release-Authority Measurement for Registry-Mediated Package Ecosystems
blurb: This paper introduces a predecessor-aware release-authority record that flags when a package's public publishing path (publisher, repository, workflow, provenance, signing) changes from the prior release, surfacing review cues that dependency graphs miss. Across 43,100 predecessor comparisons spanning npm, PyPI, Maven Central, and RubyGems, the rules identified 204 release-path discontinuities, and practitioners rated 20 of 30 sampled triggers as warranting immediate review. The signals don't catch same-path compromise or unchanged compromised CI, which need separate evidence.

title: Long-Theorized GPS Weakness Exploited on Large Scale
blurb: A large-scale jamming and spoofing campaign against GPS/GNSS signals has put long-known weaknesses of the technology under real-world stress, exploiting the fact that signals arriving from satellites 20,000 km up are weak enough to overpower or forge. One proposed countermeasure is Xona's LEO satellite Pulsar-0, which transmits positioning and timing signals roughly 100 times stronger than standard GNSS and cross-checks against GPS, making it substantially harder to jam or spoof.

title: What You See Is Not What You Execute: Memory-Based Runtime SBOM Generation for Supply Chain Security
blurb: MEM-SBOM is a memory forensics framework that generates software bills of materials directly from the runtime state of Python applications, recovering loaded modules from the interpreter's internal structures without requiring prior instrumentation. Built as Volatility 3 plugins and tested against 51 real-world apps, it hit 100% extraction accuracy and surfaced runtime packages that metadata- and filesystem-based SBOM tools miss. The payoff for defenders is a forensically sound view of what code actually executed during incident response, including which dependencies reach vulnerable functions.

title: Trump signs orders to build a quantum computer and protect against the one that could break encryption
blurb: The White House issued twin executive orders directing federal agencies to both build a large-scale quantum computer and harden systems against quantum-enabled attacks on encryption. EO 14409 mandates a post-quantum cryptography migration with key establishment secured by 2030, citing the "harvest now, decrypt later" threat where adversaries collect encrypted data today to crack once capable machines exist.

title: An Automated Framework for Input Alphabet Construction in Stateful Protocol Implementation Learning
blurb: This paper introduces an automated framework that uses LLMs to generate input alphabets for state machine learning of stateful protocol implementations, parsing message layouts and mutating them to cover valid and invalid message spaces without manual protocol expertise. A mini-batch incremental strategy reuses learned automata as the alphabet grows, and the approach reproduced known vulnerabilities and surfaced novel semantic bugs that developers have since confirmed and patched.

title: Five Eyes spooks warn AI means infosec incidents can become ‘major operational and financial crises’
blurb: Five Eyes cyber agencies issued joint guidance warning that AI is accelerating both the speed and scale of security incidents, turning routine breaches into "major operational and financial crises" for unprepared organizations. The advisory urges boards and security leaders to treat AI-driven attack acceleration as a governance issue, building faster detection and response capacity rather than relying on perimeter controls alone.

title: Récolter aujourd’hui, déchiffrer demain : pourquoi Washington accélère sa transition post-quantique
blurb: Trump signed two Executive Orders accelerating US quantum strategy, including one that moves the federal deadline for migrating to post-quantum cryptography forward from 2035 to 2031 and prioritizes hardening critical infrastructure. The compressed timeline reflects the "Harvest Now, Decrypt Later" threat, in which adversaries collect encrypted traffic today to break it once quantum decryption matures, so agencies should begin inventorying and rotating vulnerable cryptography now.

title: OpenAI launches new initiative to help find and patch open source bugs
blurb: OpenAI launched Patch the Planet, an initiative partnering with Trail of Bits to help open source maintainers find and fix vulnerabilities using its Codex Security tooling. Trail of Bits engineers will triage findings and develop patches before they reach maintainers, positioning the effort as a defensive counterpoint to AI bug-hunting tools like Anthropic's Mythos.

title: Duo accused of role in TfL cyber attack plead guilty after ‘lengthy, highly complex, and painstaking investigation’
blurb: Two alleged Scattered Spider members, Thalha Jubair, 20, and Owen Flowers, 18, pleaded guilty to Computer Misuse Act offenses over the September 2024 Transport for London attack that disrupted service for around 10 million riders and forced password resets for all 28,000 employees. NCA evidence tied Flowers to intrusions at US healthcare firms SSM Health and Sutter Health, plus video he recorded of Jubair accessing TfL systems.

title: Public Diffusion Models, Private Images: Key-Controlled Inversion for Conditional Reconstruction
blurb: This paper proposes key-controlled inversion, an access-control scheme for white-box diffusion models where published parameters let anyone invert intermediate latents back to the original input image. The authors turn diffusion's exponential error propagation into a defense by injecting key-dependent noise into the inversion formula, so only the correct key reconstructs the image and any other key yields unrecognizable output. They prove the resulting ciphertext distribution is IND-CPA secure with adversary advantage exponentially small in a tunable security parameter.

title: Towards Robust Personalized Federated Learning: Vulnerability Assessment and Defense Co-Design
blurb: This paper presents the first systematic study of adversarial threats in personalized federated learning (PFL), showing that PFL's per-client models are more exposed to transfer-based adversarial attacks than centralized training. A malicious client can use its own local model to craft adversarial examples that degrade peers' personalized models, causing significant accuracy drops across benchmark datasets. The authors propose a defense combining stochastic input noise, input-scaled trace regularization, and parameter sensitivity maximization to harden FL deployments.

title: VCT: A Verifiable Transcript System for LLM Conversations
blurb: This paper introduces VCT (Verifiable Conversation Transcript), a cryptographic scheme that makes LLM chat logs tamper-evident across non-linear operations like re-prompting, regeneration, deletion, and multi-device sync. It uses a three-tier structure (branch hash chains, session Merkle roots, account roots with joint user-server signatures) plus a gossip protocol so devices can detect server-side view forks and produce non-repudiable forensic evidence. A Python prototype adds 0.9% storage overhead and sub-millisecond crypto latency, making it viable for compliance auditing on production platforms.

title: Canadian Electricity Provider London Hydro Discloses Data Breach
blurb: London Hydro, the electricity distributor serving roughly 170,000 customers in London, Ontario, disclosed on June 20 that attackers breached its systems and likely accessed customer data, including names, addresses, email addresses, phone numbers, and account details such as billing and meter numbers. The utility says no financial data, government IDs, or dates of birth were exposed; no group has claimed the attack, and customers should watch for account-themed phishing.

title: A quantum algorithm for one-shot signatures
blurb: This paper presents a circuit-level implementation of an efficient one-shot signature scheme, where a classical public key pairs with a quantum secret key that can sign a message exactly once before being consumed. The construction prepares a superposition over a random affine coset derived from a puncturable pseudorandom function, with logical qubits scaling as Θ(κ log r + n + l) and gate complexity as Θ(n³ + nl). The authors note their security depends on circuit obfuscation, which they leave unimplemented.

title: The EVerest Dataset for Secure Software Engineering
blurb: EVerest, a multi-artifact security dataset spanning requirements, architecture, and code for EV charging stations, whose creation surfaced and fixed a CWE-1295 weakness.

title: Cross-National Information Attacks: A Two-Decade Analysis of Troll Behavior in Korea
blurb: This paper introduces an explainable ML framework for detecting state-linked troll activity, applied to 112M Korean news comments over nearly 20 years to flag 23,998 accounts consistent with coordinated manipulation. The authors find these accounts rely on morally condemning rhetoric, mostly targeting domestic political figures on both left and right, rather than overtly pushing foreign narratives, and that this framing draws significantly higher engagement.

title: Quantum Key Distribution Without Shared Reference Frame Under Unital Noise
blurb: This paper shows that secure quantum key distribution is possible over satellite links where the two parties share no common reference frame, a setup that breaks standard QKD assumptions due to relative motion and rotation. The authors model the unknown unital qubit channel via its Pauli transfer matrix and prove that BB84 and six-state protocols remain optimal under these conditions. A second sequential-basis-matching method reaches the same key rate without reconstructing the full channel.

title: CITADEL: CSI-Based Jamming Detection and Open-Set Classification for IIoT Networks
blurb: CITADEL detects and classifies IIoT RF jamming, including zero-day attacks, using only commodity-device CSI, hitting 97.1% zero-day detection.

title: Trump Signs Executive Order Accelerating Post-Quantum Cryptography Migration
blurb: Trump signed Executive Order 14409 directing OMB, NIST, NSA, DHS, and CISA to develop technical guidance that moves federal agencies to post-quantum cryptography, with deadlines of December 31, 2030 for key establishment and 2031 for digital signatures. Federal contractors must comply with NIST PQC standards by the end of 2030, making cryptographic inventory and migration a near-term procurement requirement rather than a long-horizon planning exercise.

title: Trump signs executive orders setting 2031 deadline for post-quantum migration
blurb: Trump signed executive orders requiring all federal high-value assets and systems to migrate to post-quantum cryptography by the end of 2031. The mandate forces agencies and their vendors to inventory cryptographic dependencies and budget for PQC upgrades now, pulling forward procurement timelines for security and infrastructure suppliers serving the federal market.

title: Russian Initial Access Broker Behind FortiBleed Campaign
blurb: SOCRadar reported that a Russian initial access broker is behind FortiBleed, a multi-vendor credential-harvesting campaign that has compromised exposed FortiGate firewalls since February using a custom Golang sniffer to capture authentication traffic, with more than 110 million credentials stolen across 650 harvesting pipelines. Over 430,000 FortiGate appliances fall within scope and 19,000 remain actively sniffed, exposing enterprises and the MSPs that manage Fortinet devices to identity-layer and supply-chain compromise.

title: OpenAI rolls out AI-led push to fix open-source software flaws
blurb: OpenAI launched Patch the Planet, a program with Trail of Bits that uses AI-assisted vulnerability research plus human review to find and fix flaws in core open-source projects including Python, Go, cURL, and pyca/cryptography. The effort has identified hundreds of issues and merged dozens of patches so far, though analysts caution it shifts enterprise supply-chain risk only as an input to a broader program, not a replacement.

title: Oracle sheds 21,000 roles over the past year amid wave of AI layoffs from tech giants
blurb: Oracle cut 21,000 jobs over the past year, nearly 13% of its workforce, ending May 2026 at 141,000 employees as it ties the reductions to AI adoption in its annual filing. The cuts come alongside a 162% jump in capex to $55.7B and negative $23.7B free cash flow, signaling Oracle is reallocating spend from headcount to AI infrastructure under investor pressure over its debt-funded buildout.

title: A Chinese supercomputer just secured first place on the Top500 list for the first time since 2017: Here’s everything we know about LineShine
blurb: China's LineShine supercomputer took the top spot on the 2026 Top500 list at 2.198 exaflops, ending El Capitan's run and marking the first Chinese system at number one since Sunway TaihuLight in 2017. The CPU-only cluster runs roughly 14 million Arm-based cores on a domestic stack, including LX2 processors linked to Huawei, the Kylin OS, and a proprietary interconnect, underscoring China's push to build sovereign HPC without US silicon.

title: WhatsApp VBScript Campaign Uses Fake Documents to Install ManageEngine RMM Tool
blurb: Kaspersky uncovered an active campaign that hijacks WhatsApp accounts to spread obfuscated VBScript files disguised as financial documents, which install legitimate ManageEngine RMM software to give attackers remote access to victim systems. Targeting WhatsApp Web and Desktop users across Malaysia, Brazil, India, and eight other countries, the operation abuses trusted RMM tooling to evade detection, a tactic IT teams must account for in endpoint monitoring and software-allowlisting policies.

title: Oracle Sheds 21,000 Jobs as It Sharpens Focus on AI
blurb: Oracle cut roughly 21,000 jobs in its last fiscal year, shrinking head count about 13% as it redirects spending toward AI infrastructure. The cuts signal that Oracle is funding its capital-intensive AI buildout partly through payroll, a trade-off enterprise buyers should weigh against the company's ability to support its database and cloud commitments.

title: Overestimating AI threatens legacy mainframe migrations
blurb: Gartner projected that more than two-thirds of enterprise efforts to modernize legacy mainframe systems with AI will fail, causing operational disruptions and added technical debt. The warning tells IT leaders to scope AI's role in migration realistically, treating it as one tool within a broader plan rather than a substitute for proven modernization methods and staged execution.

title: Five Eyes spooks warn AI means infosec incidents can become ‘major operational and financial crises’
blurb: The Five Eyes intelligence agencies issued joint guidance warning that AI-accelerated attacks can turn routine infosec incidents into major operational and financial crises, urging boards to treat cyber resilience as a business-continuity problem rather than an IT line item. The guidance pushes enterprises to fold AI-driven threat scenarios into incident-response plans and tabletop exercises, raising the bar for the security tooling and governance buyers are expected to procure.

title: Duo accused of role in TfL cyber attack plead guilty after ‘lengthy, highly complex, and painstaking investigation’
blurb: Two alleged members of the Scattered Spider cybercrime group, Thalha Jubair, 20, and Owen Flowers, 18, pleaded guilty to Computer Misuse Act offenses tied to the September 2024 Transport for London attack that disrupted services for months and affected an estimated 10 million people. The same collective has claimed attacks on Marks & Spencer, the Co-op, and MGM Resorts, underscoring the enterprise risk from UK-based threat actors targeting identity and access systems.

title: Change your cyber risk strategy to meet AI threats, Five Eyes countries warn CSOs
blurb: The Five Eyes cybersecurity agencies, including CISA, the UK's NCSC, and Canada's CCCS, urged CSOs to rewrite their cyber risk strategies as threat actors increasingly use AI to find and exploit vulnerabilities faster. The agencies framed cyber resilience as a board-level business risk and pushed foundational controls, though some experts called the guidance too general and too late to help.

title: Chevron signs 20-year power agreement with Microsoft for West Texas data center
blurb: Chevron's Energy Forge One subsidiary signed a 20-year power purchase agreement with Microsoft to build Project Kilby, a co-located West Texas facility delivering roughly 2.67GW of dedicated natural-gas generation to a Microsoft data center. The deal hands Microsoft scalable, grid-isolated power for AI compute without waiting on regional utility capacity, and pushes hyperscalers further toward private generation deals as electricity becomes the binding constraint on cloud buildout.

title: What 20 years of AWS taught me about agentic AI
blurb: Spec-driven development keeps AI coding agents from wandering, argues a 20-year AWS engineer: instead of dropping an agent into a repo with a vague prompt, co-create a requirements spec, design document, and task breakdown in Markdown first. The implication for IT leaders is that agentic coding scales to critical codebases only with the same governance discipline as human teams, not autonomy. Acceptance criteria and strict testing become the contract that defines "done.

title: Shipping huggingface_hub every week with AI, open tools, and a human in the loop
blurb: Hugging Face documented how it moved huggingface_hub from a release every 4-6 weeks to a weekly release driven by a single GitHub Actions workflow. The team automated the mechanical steps (version bumps, tagging, downstream test branches, post-release PRs) and used open-weights models to draft release notes and announcements, keeping a human in the loop only where judgment matters. The whole setup runs on open-source tools with no vendor contract or closed model required.

title: They sold their startup to Datadog. Now, they’ve raised $35M to disrupt it
blurb: Two former Datadog engineers raised $35M in Series A funding for Tsuga, a Paris-based observability platform that runs inside the customer's own cloud account on AWS to avoid the per-byte fees that make legacy tools expensive for high-telemetry AI workloads. Six months after launching, Tsuga reports several million in revenue and six-figure average contracts with customers including Black Forest Labs and Le Monde, bringing total funding to $45M.

title: Polymarket has flooded social media with deceptive videos by paid creators
blurb: Polymarket has paid social-media creators to post videos that present its prediction-market bets as organic opinions, blurring the line between sponsored content and authentic commentary across TikTok and X. The tactic puts marketers on notice that undisclosed paid creator content invites FTC scrutiny and platform enforcement, raising the compliance bar for any brand running influencer campaigns at scale.

title: Expanding financial advertiser verification across Europe
blurb: Google is expanding its financial services advertiser verification program to all 27 EU member states plus the EEA, adding 24 countries to a program already live in 18 markets and requiring financial advertisers to verify authorization against national regulators within 30 days or face restricted ads. The framework has already blocked or removed 327.8 million unauthorized financial ads globally, so finance marketers running Google Ads in Europe should budget verification lead time before launching new campaigns.

title: Hallucinations in Organization-backed AI advisors: Evidence about Skepticism, Verification, and Reliance in Goal-Directed Use
blurb: This review surveys empirical studies on whether users catch hallucinations from organization-deployed AI advisors, distinguishing four constructs that prior work conflates: skepticism, verification, whether verification succeeds, and whether it changes reliance. The most deployable interventions for organizations, generic and specific hallucination warnings, show the weakest and most mixed effects. For PMs shipping AI advisory features, the takeaway is that a "responses may be inaccurate" disclaimer is the least reliable safeguard, and that teams should measure verification separately from reliance.

title: US Senate passes housing supply bill featuring CBDC ban in 85-5 vote
blurb: The US Senate passed a housing supply bill in an 85-5 vote that includes a provision banning the Federal Reserve from issuing a central bank digital currency or any similar digital asset through the end of 2030. The CBDC prohibition, attached to unrelated housing legislation, marks the first time such a ban has cleared a full chamber of Congress rather than dying in committee.

title: OptChain: Achieving Optimal Throughput of Permissionless Blockchains
blurb: OptChain is a permissionless blockchain SMR protocol that the authors claim is the first to approach a theoretical upper bound on throughput under fixed error probability. It combines Shardis, a verifiable information dispersal mechanism that pushes intra-shard throughput to the fastest node's bandwidth limit, with diffusion mining, which keeps each shard secure as long as it holds one honest node. The paper backs the design with a formal security analysis and an AWS EC2 prototype that outperforms existing permissionless protocols.

title: SoFi's Bank-Issued Stablecoin Hits $150M as Bullish Becomes First CEX to List It
blurb: SoFi Bank's SOFIUSD crossed $150M in circulating supply on Tuesday, the same day Bullish became the first centralized exchange to list the token on its central limit order book. The listing routes institutional order flow to the first stablecoin issued by a US national bank, which is fully reserved, redeemable 1:1 for dollars, and now live on both Ethereum and Solana.

title: Baillie Gifford Launches UK-Regulated Tokenized Bond Fund on Solana and Ethereum With BNY
blurb: Baillie Gifford launched $BAGEY, a UK-regulated tokenized corporate bond fund issued natively on Solana and Ethereum with BNY handling tokenization and wallet infrastructure, targeting roughly 7% annual yield through active fixed-income management. Unlike wrapper-based tokenized funds, the token itself is the legal holding, with subscriptions and redemptions settling in USDC on-chain and the blockchain serving as the register of record.

title: Trump signs executive orders setting 2031 deadline for post-quantum migration
blurb: Trump signed executive orders requiring all federal high-value assets and systems to migrate to post-quantum cryptography by the end of 2031. The mandate sets a concrete federal timeline for quantum-resistant standards, pressuring crypto protocols and custody providers that rely on ECDSA and similar signature schemes to plan their own migrations ahead of the threat.

title: Synthetix Governance Votes to Retire sUSD, Pay Holders in Vested SNX
blurb: Synthetix governance moved to retire its sUSD stablecoin under SIP-423, freezing the contract and repaying holders four vested SNX per sUSD at a $0.25 SNX valuation, subject to a one-year lock and one-year linear vest. The token trades near $0.25 against its $1.00 target, down roughly 61% over the past 30 days, with about $17.5M in circulating supply.

title: Treasury Sanctions Three Individuals and Six Entities for Routing Crypto to ISIS
blurb: The US Treasury's Office of Foreign Assets Control sanctioned three individuals and six entities on June 22 for moving ISIS funds through money service businesses and crypto across Europe, the Middle East, and West Africa, including a Syria-based bitcoin exchange and two TRON wallets tied to a designated French national. The action is a rare OFAC designation that names specific on-chain addresses at the individual-wallet level, signaling tighter enforcement against crypto rails used for terror financing.

title: Trump signs orders to build a quantum computer and protect against the one that could break encryption
blurb: The White House issued twin executive orders directing the Department of Energy to host at least one large-scale quantum computer and requiring federal agencies to complete a post-quantum cryptography migration by 2030. The defensive order cites the "harvest now, decrypt later" threat, in which adversaries store encrypted data today to decrypt once quantum machines can break the elliptic-curve cryptography securing Bitcoin and most chains.

title: Micron Stock Goes On-Chain 48 Hours Before Earnings as Backpack Securities Lists $MU on Solana
blurb: Backpack Securities listed tokenized Micron stock ($MU) on Solana through Sunrise, 48 hours before Micron's June 24 earnings call, with each token backed 1:1 by a custodied share and redeemable through Backpack Exchange. Unlike NYSE-listed MU, the on-chain token trades continuously through the after-hours report and overnight, offered under Regulation S to non-US persons only.

title: Ripple targets EU, wins preliminary MiCA approval from Luxembourg financial regulator
blurb: Ripple won preliminary approval for a CASP license from Luxembourg's CSSF under the EU's MiCA framework, clearing a path to offer its stablecoin payment systems across the bloc and expand into broader crypto services. The single-state approval passports across all EU members, though it lands as the European Commission opens a consultation over whether MiCA's stablecoin rules, including a ban on yield and steep cash-reserve requirements, remain fit for purpose.

title: Crypto in 2026: Oh, This Is the Bad Place
blurb: Stephen Diehl argues that 2026 crypto has normalized a set of dystopian developments: a sitting US president running a memecoin from the White House with top holders seated by purchase volume, a licensed exchange taking retail bets on foreign-leader assassinations, and a federally sanctioned stablecoin system migrating poor savers' money onto a few opaque private balance sheets. He contends that policy vocabulary now has to update to describe a landscape that no longer registers as abnormal.

title: David Bailey’s Nakamoto closes legacy healthcare clinics as bitcoin operating company pivot nears completion
blurb: Nakamoto, the bitcoin treasury company led by David Bailey, closed its legacy healthcare clinics on June 19, completing a merger-linked wind-down of the operations it inherited through its reverse merger. The shutdown clears the way for the company to operate purely as a bitcoin holding and acquisition vehicle, consolidating its balance sheet around its BTC reserve strategy.

title: MoneyGram Becomes Solana Validator, Joins Institutional Developer Platform
blurb: MoneyGram launched an active Solana validator node and joined Solana's institutional developer platform, its first direct participation in Solana consensus and third validator position after Tempo and Cardano's Midnight sidechain. The move follows MoneyGram's June 2 launch of MGUSD, a USD-backed stablecoin issued on Stellar via Bridge, extending a payments-infrastructure strategy that has already settled over $4.2B in USDC remittance volume.

title: US Senate passes housing supply bill featuring CBDC ban in 85-5 vote
blurb: The US Senate passed a housing supply bill in an 85-5 vote that includes a provision banning the Federal Reserve from issuing a CBDC or any similar digital asset through the end of 2030. The lopsided margin signals durable bipartisan resistance to a US central bank digital currency, leaving private stablecoins and existing payment rails as the default vehicles for digital-dollar settlement.

title: SoFi's Bank-Issued Stablecoin Hits $150M as Bullish Becomes First CEX to List It
blurb: SoFi Bank's SOFIUSD stablecoin crossed $150M in circulating supply the same day Bullish became the first centralized exchange to list it, opening institutional order-flow access outside the SoFi consumer app. Issued by nationally chartered SoFi Bank, N.A. and redeemable 1:1, the token now spans Ethereum and Solana and settles across Mastercard's Multi-Token Network for card transactions.

title: Baillie Gifford Launches UK-Regulated Tokenized Bond Fund on Solana and Ethereum With BNY
blurb: Baillie Gifford launched $BAGEY, a UK-regulated tokenized corporate bond fund issued natively on Solana and Ethereum with BNY handling tokenization and wallet infrastructure, targeting roughly 7% annual yield. The token is the legal holding rather than a wrapped receipt, so subscriptions and redemptions settle in USDC directly on-chain, replacing the transfer-agent layer and T+2 processing with blockchain-speed settlement under an FCA-regulated structure.

title: Micron Stock Goes On-Chain 48 Hours Before Earnings as Backpack Securities Lists $MU on Solana
blurb: Sunrise and Backpack Securities listed tokenized Micron stock ($MU) on Solana, each token backed 1:1 by a real share held in custody and redeemable through Backpack Exchange, 48 hours ahead of Micron's June 24 earnings call. Unlike the equity itself, $MU trades and settles continuously through the after-hours print and overnight, though Regulation S limits it to non-U.S. holders.

title: MoneyGram Becomes Solana Validator, Joins Institutional Developer Platform
blurb: MoneyGram launched an active validator node on Solana and joined the Solana Developer Platform, its third blockchain validator position after Tempo and Cardano's Midnight sidechain. The move follows MoneyGram's June 2 launch of MGUSD, a GENIUS Act-ready stablecoin issued on Stellar via Stripe-owned Bridge, signaling the remittance firm wants to operate the rails it settles on rather than just integrate third-party chains.

title: Ripple targets EU, wins preliminary MiCA approval from Luxembourg financial regulator
blurb: Ripple won preliminary approval for a Crypto Asset Service Provider license from Luxembourg's CSSF under MiCA, giving it a passportable path to offer stablecoin payment systems across the EU. The timing is awkward, as the European Commission opened a consultation last month over MiCA's stablecoin rules, including a ban on paying interest and reserve requirements demanding up to 60% of backing held in commercial bank deposits.
